Cook stated: "In a sense, it's un-rock 'n' roll, and kind of dull. But that was something we did consciously, in a sense of tightening problems we had on the first record." [6]
- CHVRCHES wrote more than 20 songs for Every Open Eye; Cook confirmed during the EOE tour (2015-10) that the band had "certainly a few in the bank." [6]
- Cook stated in 2015-10, during the EOE tour, that the band would not repeat the six-week post-TBOWYTB-tour break before returning to the studio: "I don't think we'll do what we did last time and take six weeks (off) and go back to the studio. As we get time or inspiration to work on an idea, we'll do that." [6]
- Cook on the EOE sound (2015-10): "It didn't take too long to find the sound that defined the record. It's like the first album, but it's a bit more focused, a bit more refined, a bit leaner, and the songwriting and production has matured a lot." [6]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Eurosonic Noorderslag, 10 January 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Eurosonic Noorderslag on 10 January 2013 at Academie Minerva in Groningen, Netherlands. [1]
- The set lasted approximately 25 minutes. [1]
- "Lies" appeared early in the set. [1]
- "The Mother We Share" was the closing song of the set. [1]
- The two non-vocalist members used synthesizers, drum machines, and a laptop during the Eurosonic performance. [1]

Caption: Every Open Eye (Album Release)
Facts:
- Every Open Eye is CHVRCHES' second studio album, released in September 2015. The album title is taken from a line in the track "Clearest Blue"; Mayberry stated it was also chosen to reflect the fact that she felt constantly watched and analyzed during the band's rise. [1,2,3,4]
- In January 2015, CHVRCHES began working on new material for Every Open Eye at Alucard Studios, which they were able to upgrade thanks to the success of The Bones of What You Believe. [2]
- The production of Every Open Eye took inspiration from Quincy Jones' lean yet massive-sounding production style; Martin Doherty cited the arrangements of "Smooth Criminal" and "Billie Jean" (each approximately 8 tracks) as a revelation that drove him to strip songs that had grown to 80 tracks back to their essentials. [2,5]
- Every Open Eye debuted at number four in the U.K. [2]
- Every Open Eye reached number eight on the Billboard 200 Albums chart in the U.S. [2,6]
- Every Open Eye became a Top Ten hit in Australia, Ireland, Scotland, and New Zealand. [2]
- Every Open Eye debuted at #1 on Billboard's Top Rock Albums and Alternative Albums charts (both dated October 17, 2015), with 34,000 copies sold; on the Alternative Albums chart, it spent 7 weeks. [6,7]
- Every Open Eye was released on 2015-09-25 in Australia on the Vertigo label. It entered the Australian album chart on 2015-10-11, peaked at #3, and spent 6 weeks on the chart — corroborating the "Top Ten hit in Australia" claim. [8]
- The Australian commercial single release dates for Every Open Eye singles, per the australian-charts.com discography, were: "Leave A Trace" (Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol, Australia 2015-07-20), "Empty Threat" (Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol, Australia 2015-11-02), and "Clearest Blue" (Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol, Australia 2016-02-29). All three carried the multi-label imprint shared by the parent album. [8]
- Every Open Eye was mixed by Spike Stent, whose credits include U2, Beyoncé, and Björk; Dave Simpson served as recording engineer. [5,9]
- By the time of the EOE release (2015), CHVRCHES had established themselves as festival regulars, having performed at Glastonbury, T in the Park, SXSW, and The Great Escape; their most recent major festival appearance at time of publication was Lollapalooza (2015). [10]
- Every Open Eye was released on 2015-09-25 via Glassnote / Virgin (as described in the SXSW 2016 official CHVRCHES artist bio); the Australian release was on Virgin's Vertigo imprint. [11,8]
- Every Open Eye debuted and peaked at #11 on the Billboard Canadian Albums chart (chart week 2015-10-17), spending 1 week on the chart. [12]
- Every Open Eye was certified Silver in the UK by the BPI on 2016-06-17, and certified Gold on 2018-09-14. [13]
- The album photography for Every Open Eye was taken at a desert location; Iain Cook recalled it as "a fun day in the desert" during the RSD 2025 unboxing video (2025-04-11). [14]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Falls Festival 2018/2019
Facts:
- CHVRCHES were confirmed as one of five headline acts for Falls Festival 2018/2019 alongside Catfish and the Bottlemen, Vance Joy, Hilltop Hoods, and Anderson .Paak & The Free Nationals; the lineup announcement was published by triple j on 30 August 2018. [1]
- Falls Festival 2018/2019 took place over New Year's at four Australian locations: Lorne (Victoria), Marion Bay (Tasmania), Byron Bay (New South Wales), and Fremantle (Western Australia). [1]
- The Lorne (Victoria) leg of Falls Festival 2018/2019 sold out, with tickets still available for the Byron Bay, Marion Bay, and Fremantle legs at the time of the article's update. [1]
- Sydney Opera House (2019-01-07) served as the final date of CHVRCHES' Australian touring run alongside Falls Festival 2018/2019; the reviewer confirms attending a separate Falls Festival show in addition to this standalone headliner. [2]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Field Day 2019, The Domain, Sydney
Facts:
- CHVRCHES were confirmed on the Field Day 2019 lineup; the lineup announcement was published by triple j on 28 August 2018. [1]
- Field Day 2019 took place on Tuesday 1 January 2019 at The Domain, Sydney, and was the festival's 18th anniversary edition. [1]
- CHVRCHES' Field Day 2019 booking was framed in the announcement as their "Australian return" following their Splendour in the Grass appearance earlier in 2018. [1]
- Tickets for Field Day 2019 went on sale on Thursday 30 August 2018. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Fuji Rock Festival, July 2018
Facts:
- CHVRCHES headlined the White Stage at Fuji Rock Festival in July 2018; the article places this before August 3, 2018, describing the "Out Of My Head" digital release as having occurred "after Fuji Rock." [1]
- CHVRCHES briefly trended at #1 on Twitter in Japan following their White Stage headline performance at Fuji Rock 2018. [1]

Caption: "Get Out" (Song)
Facts:
- "Get Out" is a single from Love Is Dead. [1]
- "Get Out" was released as a single in the US on 2018-01-31; the band's official Instagram account posted "GET OUT | OUT NOW" on that date, and an Alternative Press news report the same day described it as having "just dropped"; the Australian commercial single release followed on 2018-02-02. [2,3,6]
- The opening synth riff of "Get Out" was the first musical idea to emerge on CHVRCHES' first day in the studio with producer Greg Kurstin, as stated by the band via Alternative Press (originally sourced from Entertainment Weekly). [2]
- "Get Out" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2018-02-02 on the Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol imprint, per the australian-charts.com singles discography. [3]
- "Get Out" debuted and peaked at #17 on the Hot Rock Songs chart in February 2018. [4]
- "Get Out" peaked at #15 on the Alternative Songs airplay chart. [4]
- A Roosevelt Remix of "Get Out" was included as a Japan-exclusive bonus track on the Love Is Dead Japan Deluxe Edition (HSE-6864, Hostess Entertainment, September 2018). [5]

Caption: CHVRCHES Sign to Glassnote Records
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES signed to Glassnote Records, the same label as Mumford & Sons, in January 2013. The Under the Radar account (2015) states the signing occurred "by the end of the year" [2012], possibly reflecting late-2012 negotiations finalised in January 2013. [1,2,3]
- Co-manager Campbell McNeil floated copies of "Lies" and "Recover" to Glassnote's Daniel Glass in late 2012; Glass then travelled to the UK in November 2012 and watched CHVRCHES perform three times — two solo shows and one support show opening for Passion Pit — before offering a deal. [4]
- Glass described CHVRCHES as having "a similar quality to Phoenix — they're a band with nontraditional instruments playing rock music." [4]
- Glassnote's strategy for CHVRCHES was the same one that had made Mumford & Sons and Phoenix its flagship acts: nonstop touring in as many territories as possible. [4]
- Lauren Mayberry stated in a 2013 interview that she valued Glassnote because Daniel Glass — the person whose name is on the label — is personally and actively involved in the record label, noting this is rare in an industry where many labels "have been sold off over and over and like smashed together." [1]
- Lauren Mayberry used her legal training to read through the CHVRCHES record contract in full and provide brief notes during the signing process. [1]

Caption: "God''s Plan" (Song)
Facts:
- Martin Doherty is the primary lead vocalist on "God's Plan," consistent with the album recording; Doherty took lead vocal for the song at confirmed live performances including the Hordern Pavilion Sydney show (22 July 2018), the Margaret Court Arena Melbourne show (23 July 2018), the Ogden Theatre Denver show (7 August 2018), and LKA Longhorn Stuttgart (11 November 2018), with Mayberry in a supporting role. [1,2,3,4]
- "God's Plan" was self-produced by CHVRCHES — one of three tracks on Love Is Dead not produced with Greg Kurstin; the other two self-produced tracks were "My Enemy" (feat. Matt Berninger) and "Really Gone." [5]

Caption: "Good Girls" (CHVRCHES Single, December 2021)
Facts:
- "Good Girls" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2021-12-06 on Sacred Bones Records, per the australian-charts.com singles discography; the release on Sacred Bones (an independent label typically associated with experimental and post-punk acts) rather than Vertigo Berlin distinguishes this from the other Screen Violence-era singles. [1]
- Mayberry described "Good Girls" as: "could be viewed as political, because of what it's talking about, but it's being talked about from a personal standpoint, you know? We're not trying to write big message songs." [2]
- In August 2021, "Good Girls" was described as the current new single ahead of the Screen Violence album release on 2021-08-27; the Australian commercial single release date of 2021-12-06 suggests a later commercial window. [3]
- Mayberry described "Good Girls" thematically as a song about negotiating for the right to exist as a woman: "as a woman, you're constantly having to negotiate for your right to exist. I feel like I've done a lot of that negotiating in my time and it didn't make a fucking bit of difference." [3]
- Mayberry described "Good Girls" as "a nursery rhyme that slowly gets more fucked up," full of twinkling synths and the band's signature choppy vocals. [3]
- The Daily Telegraph described "Good Girls" as feeling "like a companion to The Cure classic Boys Don't Cry" — a journalistic characterisation noting the sonic resemblance. [3]
- The opening line of "Good Girls" — "killing your idols is such a chore" — was written after a pub conversation Mayberry participated in about whether it is acceptable to continue listening to a certain artist following allegations of sexual assault; she stated the song tackles cancel culture. [3]
- Screen Violence was the first CHVRCHES album on which Mayberry wrote lyrics without holding herself back; she stated that a few years earlier she would not have written the direct lyrics of "Good Girls" and "definitely wouldn't have wanted to keep them in because it would just feel like another stick to beat you with." [3]
- Mayberry articulated her reasoning for not self-censoring the "Good Girls" lyrics: "the only reason you would edit it out is because you're scared about potentially alienating the fans. But, at no point will a male lyricist ever have worried that talking about their life and their experiences would alienate female fans so, in the name of equality, let's just shut our eyes and see what happens." [3]

Caption: "Graffiti" (Song)
Facts:
- "Graffiti" received its first ever live performance at the opening night of the Love Is Dead tour at Elsewhere, Brooklyn, in May 2018 (described as a Saturday). Lauren Mayberry confirmed: "we only played it on Saturday for the first time." (www.youtube.com-watch-v-4nDlrS9_u80)
- "Graffiti" is the opening (first) track on Love Is Dead. (aaabackstage.com-live-review-chvrches-hordern-pavilion-22-07-2018)
- "Graffiti" debuted at #36 on the Hot Rock Songs chart (dated June 9, 2018). (www.billboard.com-pro-chvrches-love-is-dead-leads-top-rock-albums-alternative-albums)
- "Graffiti" was performed at Sydney Opera House on 2019-01-07 and was well-received by the audience; the reviewer described it as "newest single" at that time. (amnplify.com.au-portfolio-items-chvrches-sydney-opera-house-07-01-2019-live-review)
- Mayberry described the theme of "Graffiti" during Love Is Dead promotion (circa 2018): "Growing up and realizing things aren't going to be the way you thought they would be. The feeling of Stand By Me, 'I never had any friends later on like the ones I had when I was 12. Jesus, did you?'" [1,2]

Caption: "Gun" (Song)
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- "Gun" is track 3 on The Bones of What You Believe. [1]
- "Gun" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2013-06-14 on Vertigo, per the australian-charts.com singles discography. [2]
- Lauren Mayberry stated on the record that "Gun" is not about a relationship, despite widespread listener interpretation to the contrary: "A lot of people think 'Gun' is about a relationship, but it's not. But I think that's the great thing about it. What they meant to me isn't what they'll mean to all when they hear them." [1]
- Mayberry stated in 2023 that the Glasgow-specific slang behind the song was not widely understood: "I was under the misunderstanding that somebody being a gun — being a bit of a weapon — was a thing that was commonly understood, but I don't think it was widely spread. I think it must've been hyperlocal slang." She described not having researched whether the phrase would be understood outside that context. [3]
- Mayberry reflected in 2023 that "Gun" — and similar tracks across the CHVRCHES catalogue — represents aspirational take-no-shit feminism that "belies what was actually being experienced and what was actually occurring": "Songwriting should all be grounded in a place of truth, but it doesn't have to be grounded in a place of fact." [3]
- Iain Cook offered a view on the song's open-ended meaning: "Once a song becomes part of the public consciousness, it doesn't matter what the artist intended anymore, because it doesn't belong to the artist." [1]
- Due to Lauren Mayberry's Glaswegian pronunciation of the word "back," some listeners have heard a line in "Gun" as "stuck in the knife that you held up my butt" — acknowledged by Mayberry as an ongoing misheard lyric. [3]

Caption: CHVRCHES Hansa Session EP, Late 2018
Facts:
- The Hansa Session EP was released digitally on 2018-11-16; a limited 10" vinyl edition followed on 2018-12-14. [2,3]
- The Hansa Session EP was recorded at Hansa Studios in Berlin during the Love Is Dead European tour, despite a "hectic" schedule; Cook stated: "When we had the opportunity to record at Hansa we jumped at it even though we were in the middle of a hectic tour." [1,2,3]
- The Hansa Session EP tracklist comprises: "Graffiti," "Miracle," "Get Out," "Heaven/Hell," and "Really Gone." [2,3]
- The EP is named after Hansa Studios, Berlin, which Iain Cook described as "hallowed ground": "my heroes Bowie, Depeche, Iggy, Siouxsie, Tangerine Dream, Nick Cave amongst others made arguably their best work in that building." [1,2,3]
- Cook described the Hansa Session as "a chance to hold a mirror up to these five songs and look at them from a different angle, and that is something that you don't always get the chance to do as an artist." [2]
- Mayberry described the opportunity: "It was really fun to try to reinterpret the songs and still communicate the messages and emotions with different arrangements." She also noted: "Once an album is out and you're on the road, it's harder to be creative so it's exciting when those opportunities present themselves." [2,3]
- The Hansa Session EP features acoustic arrangements with string quartet for several songs from Love Is Dead. [1,3]

Caption: "He Said She Said" (CHVRCHES Single, April 2021)
Facts:
- "He Said She Said" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2021-04-19 on Vertigo Berlin, per the australian-charts.com singles discography; by Australian release date it appears to have been the lead single ahead of Screen Violence (August 2021), preceding "How Not To Drown" (Australian single release 2021-06-04) by approximately six weeks. [1]
- "He Said She Said" was explicitly identified by CHVRCHES as their first single from Screen Violence at the time of the album's announcement on 2021-06-03. [2]
- "He Said She Said" reached #1 on the NACC radio singles chart in the US. [3]
- "He Said She Said" charted in the J-WAVE TOKIO HOT 100 top 10 for three consecutive weeks in Japan. [3]

Caption: "Heaven/Hell" (Song)
Facts:
- "Heaven/Hell" appears on Love Is Dead (2018). A Love Is Dead promotional text describes the song as addressing what it means to be a rock band with a female frontwoman. The promotional text quotes the following lyrics: "Is it right if I'm a perfect actress / Playing the princess in distress? / Is it alright if I save myself and / If I clean up my own mess? / Is it enough yet, 'cause I've had enough?" [1,2]
- Lauren Mayberry described the subject matter of "Heaven/Hell" in Love Is Dead promotional material (circa 2018, translated from German): the song addresses the vulgar sexism and misogyny she encounters — and is expected to tolerate — because she is a woman singing on stage. [1,2]

Caption: "Here with Me" (Marshmello featuring CHVRCHES, 2019)
Facts:
- In 2019, CHVRCHES appeared on Marshmello's track "Here with Me." [1]
- "Here With Me (Marshmello feat. Chvrches)" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2019-03-08 on the Joytime / Republic multi-label imprint. It entered the Australian singles chart on 2019-03-31, peaked at #9, and spent 17 weeks on the chart. [2]
- "Here with Me" (Marshmello ft. CHVRCHES) was certified Silver in the UK by the BPI on 2019-05-09, certified Gold on 2019-07-18, and certified Platinum on 2020-04-02; the Platinum is the highest UK certification of any CHVRCHES-associated release in the BPI database. [3]
- The BPI lists "Here with Me" under corporate label UNIVERSAL MUSIC JOYTIME/POSITIVA, distinct from all other CHVRCHES entries in the BPI database which carry UNIVERSAL MUSIC VIRGIN. [3]
- "Here With Me" accumulated more than 15 million views on YouTube (figure stated in an August 2021 interview). [4]
- Mayberry described "Here With Me" as CHVRCHES' "most successful song ever" in August 2021. [4]

Caption: "High Enough to Carry Me Over" (Song)
Facts: - Martin Doherty is the primary lead vocalist on "High Enough to Carry You Over" (also referred to as "High Enough to Carry Me Over" in some live-review sources), consistent with the album recording; a 2015 Billboard review by Carl Wilson also identified this as the song where "one of the guys occasionally takes over lead vocals." At the 22 July 2018 Hordern Pavilion show, Doherty took lead vocal on the track live. [1,2,3]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Hostess Club Weekender, Japan (TBOWYTB Era)
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts: - CHVRCHES performed at Hostess Club Weekender in Japan; the Hostess Entertainment product page (September 2018) — which is published by the same company that organises the Weekender series — mentions this as part of their breakthrough in Japan following the release of The Bones of What You Believe in September 2013. The specific edition(s) of Hostess Club Weekender they appeared at are not stated in this source. [1]

Caption: "How Not to Drown" (Song)
Facts:
- "How Not to Drown" features Robert Smith alongside Lauren Mayberry on vocals; AllMusic's Heather Phares describes the track as Mayberry and Smith "trading notes on self-preservation." [1]
- "How Not to Drown" was identified as the second single from Screen Violence at the time of the album's announcement on 2021-06-03. [2]
- "How Not To Drown (Chvrches feat. Robert Smith)" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2021-06-04 on Vertigo Berlin, per the australian-charts.com singles discography. [3]
- CHVRCHES described their experience collaborating with Robert Smith on "How Not To Drown" as "mental" in a contemporaneous NME interview at the time of the single's 2021 release. [4]
- The collaboration came about through CHVRCHES' manager, who knew someone in common with Robert Smith and passed on the band's email address; Smith has no manager and communicates only by email. The first message CHVRCHES received from Smith said "Aloha." Mayberry described the collaboration as "insane." [5]
- All work on "How Not to Drown" was done remotely, exclusively by email, with no Zoom or in-person sessions; Mayberry stated: "We sent him a bunch of demos we'd been working on not knowing what to expect. It was all worked on remotely, just through email, no Zoom or anything." [5]
- Mayberry described the band's feeling about the Robert Smith collaboration: "If everything ended tomorrow, we'd be beyond happy." [5]
- Martin Doherty recounted on ALT 92.3 (original broadcast 2021-07-04) that CHVRCHES' manager initiated the collaboration by attempting to contact Robert Smith's management without realising that Smith is self-managed; the manager then received a direct message from Smith asking what they wanted, which he relayed to the band in a state of excitement. Smith confirmed on the same broadcast that his message was words "along the lines of, 'What do you want?'" This account complements the earlier detail (from The Skinny) that Smith subsequently communicated by email and that CHVRCHES' first direct message from him opened with "Aloha." [6]
- Robert Smith stated on ALT 92.3 (2021-07-04) that he received a batch of five or six songs from CHVRCHES that were already "pretty well-formed" — "they sounded like proper songs, they weren't just like sketches" — and that he wrote back to the band with individual thoughts on each one before settling on "How Not to Drown." He described walking around outside singing along to the demos: "I just felt that 'How Not to Drown' was — just sort of — it just felt right." [6]
- Robert Smith stated that the demo of "How Not to Drown" already had "a bit of a male vocal going on it," which helped him think about how he could fit into the track vocally; he also cited the song's melodic and textural qualities as drawing him to it above the other songs in the batch. [6]
- Robert Smith's only stated production note on the original mix of "How Not to Drown" was to turn up the bass, which he called "a hook." Iain Cook also referenced hi-hat adjustments; Martin Doherty confirmed the adjustment was technically complex because the elements were "all connected" in the production, resulting in a revised mix with "hats down" noted in the title. [6]
- A Robert Smith remix of "How Not to Drown" was released prior to 2021-07-04 (the broadcast date of the ALT 92.3 interview, at which the remix was described as having "just come out"). Smith described his remix process as taking individual stems from the original and highlighting elements he felt were underrepresented in the mix. [6]
- CHVRCHES described Robert Smith as their "ultimate hero" at the time of the "How Not To Drown" collaboration (translated from Japanese). [7]
- "How Not To Drown" was selected as the cover of Apple Music's "New Music Daily" playlist at the time of its 2021 release. [7]
- In live performance during the Screen Violence tour, Martin Doherty played a small piano brought down closer to the front of the stage during "How Not To Drown," while Lauren Mayberry managed both vocal parts of the duet (her own and Smith's studio part). [8]
- "How Not To Drown" was released as a digital single on 2021-06-02, one day before the formal Screen Violence album announcement (2021-06-03); the release was previewed by a premiere via Zane Lowe on Apple Music at 9am PT / 12pm ET / 5pm UK. [9]
- The original lyrics for "How Not to Drown" were different from those in the released version; both the pre-final demo (with original lyrics) and a snippet of the final vocal recording were played at the music video premiere livestream in June 2021. Martin Doherty referred to his own demo contribution as "mad autumn vocals." [10]
- The "How Not to Drown" music video was filmed entirely by Iain Cook, working alone in Scotland; Cook completed the video independently and delivered the finished product to Mayberry and Doherty, who had no on-set involvement. Mayberry described it: "He silently made the video and then delivered us the end product." [10]
- Robert Smith, at the music video premiere livestream in June 2021, said the video was "consensual" and "good because it makes you want to watch the third one." [10]
- "How Not To Drown" was ranked #8 on Consequence of Sound's Top Songs of 2021 list. [11]
- "How Not To Drown" originated as a file Martin Doherty had named "piano drum ting," written during the Love Is Dead US tour leg while he was "hiding in the most remote corner of some venue, making music on my laptop" as a means of escaping what was going on in his head. [12]
- The Robert Smith collaboration came about after CHVRCHES cold-emailed The Cure's manager about possible support slots; this initial contact opened the creative relationship that led to "How Not To Drown." (The Daily Telegraph account — "cold emailed The Cure's manager" — complements the existing corpus record, which describes CHVRCHES' manager attempting to reach Smith's management and discovering Smith is self-managed.) [12]
- The day "How Not To Drown" was released, Doherty posted on Instagram: "today is the proudest moment of my life in music. Confirmation that even when things seem like they're at their worst, something good can grow. I may never entirely conquer my mental health issues but they can be a catalyst for something positive. If you feel like giving up, don't." [12]

Caption: Iain Cook — Biographical Facts
Facts:
- Iain Cook was born on 1974-11-02.
- Iain Cook grew up in West Lothian and had lived in Glasgow for approximately 15 years as of December 2013, placing his move to Glasgow around 1998–1999. [1]
- Iain Cook stated in December 2013 that he disagreed with the view that musicians must move to London to build a career, saying "Many of them run away to London, but we're staying there" in reference to CHVRCHES remaining based in Glasgow. [1]
- Iain Cook studied at Glasgow's University of Strathclyde, where he met Martin Doherty in 2003; at the time, Cook was lecturing a class at Strathclyde in which Doherty was a student — a fact that many published profiles omitted, instead describing the two as simply having "met at university." [2,3,4]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: the shirt that caught Cook's eye — AllMusic and Consequence of Sound describe it as a "Paul Harvey t-shirt," while Stereogum (2023) identifies it as a "PJ Harvey shirt." PJ Harvey (the musician) is the more contextually plausible referent; the earlier sources may contain a transcription error. [2,3,4]
- Iain Cook's role in Aereogramme was as guitarist; he was a member of the band for approximately eight years. [5,3]
- Iain Cook originally studied architecture before switching to music; despite this background, he is described as "the most seasoned in rock" of the three CHVRCHES members. [3]
- Cook has been a member of the following Glasgow-based guitar bands prior to CHVRCHES: Les Tinglies, Aereogramme, and The Unwinding Hours. He described himself as "a veteran of Glasgow's indie rock scene for the last 20 years" (as of 2015). [6]
- After leaving Aereogramme by 2007, Cook became a composer for film and television; his composing work included work for the BBC specifically. He described his output as including "lots of adverts and a couple of children's TV series," adding that the names of the series would "remain a secret." [2,5,9]
- During his post-Aereogramme BBC composing period, Cook collaborated with Jim Kerr of Simple Minds. [5]
- Cook has openly admitted his affinity for catchy pop music, citing artists such as Katy Perry as examples. [5]
- In addition to his film and TV scoring work, Cook was a member of the Scottish post-rock band The Unwinding Hours following Aereogramme's dissolution and before forming CHVRCHES. [7]
- Iain Cook singled out the Troubadour in Los Angeles as a standout CHVRCHES live venue, describing its intimate atmosphere — the sense of the audience being all around the performers — as well as the historical photographs of Janis Joplin, Jimi Hendrix, and Jim Morrison on the walls: "This place has got some real history and you can feel it." [8]
- Cook described his TV and film composing work as "quite soul-chipping, just spending hours on the computer plodding away at the same note" (made in a Pitchfork interview, cross-referenced in the Chicago Tribune's 2015-10-22 profile). [10]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Intersect Music Festival 2019, Las Vegas
Facts:
- CHVRCHES were listed on the full lineup for the inaugural Intersect Music Festival, scheduled for 2019-12-06 and 2019-12-07 at Las Vegas Festival Grounds, Las Vegas, Nevada; the lineup was announced via official AWS press release on 2019-10-16. [1]
- The Intersect Music Festival was presented by Amazon Web Services (AWS) and produced by Production Club; it was the inaugural edition of the festival. [1]
- Co-billed acts at Intersect 2019 included Foo Fighters, Kacey Musgraves, Beck, Brandi Carlile, and Flying Lotus, among approximately 30 acts total. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES Sign to Island Records, February 2023
Facts:
- CHVRCHES signed to Island Records in February 2023, announced concurrently with the release of their single "Over"; the Billboard headline describing the event was "Chvrches Returns With Huge Single 'Over,' Island Records Deal & 'A New Lease on Life'" (Jason Lipshutz, 23 February 2023). [1]
- The signing itself occurred in 2022 — Mayberry referred to it as "last year" in the February 2023 Billboard interview — with the public announcement made concurrently with the "Over" single release on 2023-02-24. [2]
- The deal involved Island Records for North America and EMI for the UK; the Australian release of "Over" (2023-02-24) appeared on the Virgin EMI imprint, consistent with the UK/International EMI component. [2,1]
- CHVRCHES left Glassnote Records after four albums (The Bones of What You Believe, Every Open Eye, Love Is Dead, and Screen Violence) for the new deal. Mayberry specifically named Daniel Glass as the person the band is "forever indebted" to: "We're incredibly grateful to Glassnote and forever indebted to Daniel Glass for seeing something in our band and being so supportive of that over the years. That'll always be a part of the family of the band and the foundation of what it was"; she also described the broader partnership as one in which CHVRCHES were "always really lucky to have great partners." [2,3]
- Mayberry described the emotional weight of leaving Glassnote: "Making some of the changes was quite emotional… But we're really excited by what Island and EMI were bringing to the table." [2]
- Martin Doherty framed the signing as opening a new creative chapter: "Something that's come up recently, that I thought was a nice way to frame this, is that we signed new record deals, and there's kind of a new lease on life for the band. It's a chance for us to work within a new paradigm." [2]
- Mayberry cited a desire for a label that approached CHVRCHES holistically as a driver of the move: "It was really exciting to talk to people who viewed it more holistically, like, 'Where are Chvrches fans, and how can we get things to them?'" She contrasted this with what she described as an "old-school approach" of getting songs on the radio that she felt had not served the band. [2]

Caption: Julia Thirteen — Martin Doherty's First Band
Facts:
- Martin Doherty's first band was called Julia Thirteen. [1]
- Iain Cook helped Julia Thirteen record some of their first and only material; Doherty explained that the band "couldn't afford microphones" and relied on Cook's assistance. [1]
- Julia Thirteen were active before the Aereogramme dissolution in 2007; the article implies they recorded a small body of work that was never widely released. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Laneway Festival, Detroit, 14 September 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Laneway Festival in Detroit on 2013-09-14, per the June 2013 tour announcement. [1,2]
- After the CHVRCHES performance at Laneway Festival Detroit, Sigur Rós frontman Jónsi visited their dressing room to express his fandom over beers. Iain Cook described this as a "nice moment," though he was understated in recounting it. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Laneway Festival, Footscray Community Arts Centre, Melbourne, 2016-02-13
Facts:
- The 2016 Laneway Festival at Footscray Community Arts Centre, Melbourne (on the banks of the Maribyrnong River), on 2016-02-13 was sold out. [1]
- CHVRCHES performed on the Dean Turner stage and were described as one of the biggest names on the bill. [1]
- Violent Soho performed immediately before CHVRCHES on the Dean Turner stage. [1]
- CHVRCHES opened their set with "Never Ending Circles", accompanied by a spectacular lights show. [1]
- The CHVRCHES set included (partial list): "Never Ending Circles," "Gun," "Recover," "The Mother We Share"; the set was described as showcasing *Every Open Eye*. [1]
- A technical glitch left Lauren Mayberry inaudible on two separate occasions during the set; she returned each time to rapturous applause. [1]
- At the end of the set, Lauren Mayberry gave her necklace to a fan in the front row who was overcome with emotion. [1]
- Other notable acts on the 2016 Melbourne Laneway Festival bill included Flume, Purity Ring, High Tension, Violent Soho, and Hermitude. [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES on Late Night with Jimmy Fallon, June 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- In June 2013, CHVRCHES performed on Late Night with Jimmy Fallon. [1,3]
- CHVRCHES' appearance on Late Night with Jimmy Fallon was their debut US television performance. [2,3]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry at 9:30 Club, Washington, D.C., 4 September 2023
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry performed at the 9:30 Club in Washington, D.C. on 2023-09-04; NPR's All Things Considered confirmed this was her first-ever solo concert — she had previously played the 9:30 Club "many times" as part of CHVRCHES. [1,2]
- Promotional materials for the show — including the venue marquee, tour posters, and merchandise T-shirts — featured only "Lauren Mayberry" with no reference to CHVRCHES. [2]
- Audience members were lined up around the block before the doors opened. Mayberry stated: "I'm very excited and honestly baffled that people have bought tickets to these shows before there was any music." [2,3]
- The stage was decorated with bouquets of flowers. [2]
- The live band comprised musicians on drums, guitar, and keys — all women, who also joined in tight harmonies; Mayberry confirmed the all-women lineup was intentional, noting she had spent approximately 20 years performing in bands with men. [2]
- Mayberry performed a nine-song set: eight originals and one cover, a rendition of Madonna's "Like A Prayer." She addressed the crowd: "There's two depressing slow ones, and then the rest of them have a bit of pep." [2]
- Backstage before the show, Mayberry described the psychological tension between creative ambition and self-consciousness at seeing her own face on solo tour materials: "I'm gonna be sick, I can't, I can't! I'm like putting merch together and I was like, I don't think you should buy a T-shirt with my face on it — why would anybody want that? But then I can kind of switch into the other part, and I think it's important that the two exist, the yin and the yang." [2]
- The full nine-song set comprised, in order: Bird (opener, driving feel with '80s-pop-esque instrumentation); Shame; Change Shapes; Mantra; Under the Knife; Crocodile Tears; Like a Prayer; Are You Awake?; Sorry, Etc. (closer, punk-ish feel). [4]
- The "Like a Prayer" cover began with a jazzy piano intro before opening into a full synth-bop arrangement, with Mayberry hopping around the stage with a tambourine. [4]
- "Are You Awake?" was performed with Mayberry starting solo at the piano, building into what Consequence described as "an ethereal blossom of sound." [4]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry at 9:30 Club, Washington, D.C., 18 February 2025
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry performed at the 9:30 Club in Washington, D.C. on 2025-02-18 as part of the Vicious Creature Tour. [1]
- The set lasted approximately one hour and featured most of the songs from her debut solo album Vicious Creature; the performance included all 12 album tracks plus one cover. [1]
- The set included the following songs (as reported): Crocodile Tears; Change Shapes; Mantra; Shame; Anywhere but Dancing; Punch Drunk; Something in the Air; Are You Awake?; Bittersweet Symphony (The Verve cover); A Work of Fiction; Sorry, Etc; Encore: Oh, Mother; Sunday Best. [1]
- Backing musicians on the date were Heather Nation (guitar) and Marian Li-Pino (drums). [1]
- During the main set, Lauren performed a cover of The Verve's "Bittersweet Symphony" that opened with an extended solo keyboard intro with Lauren in a spotlight before the full band's entry. [1]
- Lauren used a hybrid megaphone/flashlight prop during "Something in the Air." [1]
- The main set closed with "Sorry, Etc," during which Lauren threw her microphone stand across the stage. [1]
- Lauren wore a bright glittering hat she called a "sporty doily" and a red t-shirt bearing a message of support for trans readers during the performance. [1]
- A pre-recorded/robotic announcement during the event offered a chance to win a signed setlist by supporting an LGBTQ cause. [1]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry — Biographical Facts
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry was born on 1987-10-07.
- Lauren Mayberry is 5 feet 2 inches tall and wears a UK shoe size 3. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry studied law for four years, describing the experience as "losing four years of my life inside a windowless library basement." [1]
- Lauren Mayberry grew up in rural Stirlingshire before moving to Glasgow. [2,3]
- As a child, Mayberry played piano and drums in school; singing in front of people left her feeling vulnerable and exposed, and she had no interest in the spotlight. She stated she never wanted to be the lead singer of a band. Despite having developed a commanding stage presence, she says she has never completely overcome those anxieties — occasionally getting through performances by adopting a persona she described as "20 to 30 percent tougher" than she actually feels at that moment. [4]
- Lauren Mayberry is originally from Scotland and now lives in Los Angeles. [5]
- Lauren Mayberry holds a law degree as well as a journalism degree, both from Glasgow's University of Strathclyde; AllMusic describes it as a "Master's degree in journalism," Paste Magazine (March 2013) similarly refers to "a masters in journalism," while a 2013 Consequence of Sound interview describes it as a "one-year journalism degree" — the balance of sources favours the Master's qualification. [6,7,8,25]
- In the approximately three years before forming CHVRCHES (circa 2008–2011), Lauren Mayberry worked as a freelance journalist for various local magazines and websites in Glasgow, writing about music; she had already interviewed pop stars including Kelly Rowland and The Strokes' Fabrizio Moretti before joining the band. [8,22]
- Lauren Mayberry had previously played synth and written vocal parts and lyrics with the band Blue Sky Archives, also based in Glasgow, before forming CHVRCHES; she also drummed in the group. Mayberry described Blue Sky Archives as "alternative indie with a kind of post-rocky rhythm section" and her role: "I played synth in Blue Sky Archives, and I've always been the person that would write the vocal parts or the lyrics." Her friend Ross Rankin also played in Blue Sky Archives. Iain Cook produced Blue Sky Archives' Triple A-Side EP before inviting Mayberry to sing on CHVRCHES demos. [6,9,10,11,3]
- Lauren Mayberry had a short-lived high school band called Boyfriend/Girlfriend, which hosted their "earnest rock" on MySpace in the mid-2000s. [3]
- Mayberry described herself as "the least trained person in every room we go into," having never had formal music lessons before joining CHVRCHES — whose other two members, Iain Cook and Martin Doherty, had both attended music school. Her methods of crafting songs were rooted in the DIY scene: using reference songs to communicate ideas and "scrapbooking together" the style of a verse or refrain. In December 2024 she elaborated: "They'd been to music school, and I hadn't. So I always felt like I was on the back foot, in terms of where I sat in the hierarchy." [3,16]
- Mayberry relocated to Los Angeles before the COVID-19 pandemic with her partner and two recently adopted rescue cats named Poppy and Cactus. [3]
- Mayberry had previously lived in New York, having relocated there between tours towards the end of the Every Open Eye cycle (circa 2016–2017), before later moving to Los Angeles; she noted she feels "way more foreign in Los Angeles." [12,3]
- In the early CHVRCHES recording sessions, Mayberry often attended after her day job, sitting on the couch in the back with her laptop, "half doing my day job, and half 'Can I write lyrics for that? No?'" — she described feeling self-conscious and wanting to get the band used to her presence before taking a more active creative role. In later sessions there was more space for her to write lyrics. [11]
- Mayberry wrote her first full set of lyrics for CHVRCHES — tentatively "Lungs" or "Gun" (she was uncertain which came first, possibly the same week) — while sitting in Waverley Gardens, Edinburgh, when she was there for work. [11]
- Before becoming CHVRCHES' frontwoman, Mayberry's musical background was as a drummer and keyboard player, not a vocalist; she stated: "I had been a drummer and keyboard player and never a singer, then all of a sudden I was in this position where I was 23 and being looked at and told I wasn't very good at it." [13,19]
- Mayberry has been in bands with boys and men since age 15: "Since I was 15, I've been in bands with boys and men." [13]
- Mayberry grew up in a musical household where the radio was always on; in the 1990s the household listening included Whitney Houston and Annie Lennox. She recalls a room in the family home where all the CDs and records were kept, and examining the covers and inlay artwork as a child. She mistakenly believed Steely Dan to be a woman — a "cool surfer chick that had a band" — based on a surfer girl depicted on a Steely Dan greatest hits CD cover. [14]
- As a child, Mayberry would choreograph dance routines alone in her room, but had no interest in performing them in front of others. [14]
- Mayberry learned drums because she wanted to be in a band but did not think she would be suited to guitar or to being a frontperson; she ended up as a vocalist by accident, describing the process as "People just kept taking instruments away from me." [14]
- Mayberry reviewed music for The Skinny magazine but found she could only give five-star or three-star reviews, unable to write harshly about music she didn't like; editors tried to get her to write "hard takes" on music outside her taste, but she resisted: "If you know what it's like to make something and to have that be shat on, it feels quite bad." [14]
- Mayberry cited the film Almost Famous as an early influence on her interest in music journalism: "I just watched Almost Famous and I thought, I could — that'd be great." [14]
- Mayberry stated she does not believe singing in one's natural accent should be either glorified or suppressed: "I don't think singing in your accent should be a badge of honour. I also don't think it's something you have to bury so your band will be more acceptable on radio. That's twisted." [17]
- Around December 2012, Mayberry had recently begun taking vocal lessons to build up her vocal strength. She said: "I'm pretty sure if I had done that before, I wouldn't have the horrible vowel sounds that I do!" [17]
- Mayberry quipped that her mother "cries herself to sleep" that Lauren did not pursue a legal career (said in December 2012): "It's my mother. She still cries herself to sleep that I didn't pursue a legal career!" [17]
- Mayberry stated during Love Is Dead promotion (circa 2018): "I've had to grow up in front of people." [20]
- In CHVRCHES' early live shows, Mayberry hid "behind a tambourine," having not yet fully grown into her role as a frontwoman. She later described wrestling with visibility at this stage: "I spent a certain amount of time being so uncomfortable with it, and wanting to make myself smaller and smaller and just be left alone." She ultimately chose not to let fear dictate her self-presentation: "This is my life. Do I want to constantly dress in a baggy t-shirt so that people leave me alone?... because you're making those decisions out of fear and I don't feel that, psychologically, is a good way to live your life." [18]
- A bandmate in Blue Sky Archives, Mayberry's pre-CHVRCHES band, lent her a copy of The Postal Service's *Give Up* "as an example of good girl-boy vocal sharing for us to try and emulate"; Mayberry described falling in love with the album. She named Ben Gibbard and Jenny Lewis as "probably two of my favourite lyricists of all time." [21]
- Mayberry was at university in 2008 and 2009 — contextually during the period of her law degree at the University of Strathclyde. [21]
- Lauren Mayberry won awards for her journalism work prior to forming CHVRCHES (cited in a 2013 Billboard profile; specific awards not named in the source). [23]
- The Chicago Tribune (2015-10-22) characterised Mayberry's voice as recalling "British rock stars Kate Bush or Harriet Wheeler of the Sundays" — a vocal comparison unique to this source in the corpus; no other documented source names Harriet Wheeler of The Sundays as a reference point for Mayberry's voice. [24]
- Mayberry described her law training as practically useful within CHVRCHES for proofreading the band's agreements and providing highlighted copies to Cook and Doherty: "the law thing is helpful when proofreading agreements and giving you two guys highlighted copies." Doherty confirmed: "When you say helpful, it just means that Iain and I don't have to read them." [25]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry at Brighton Music Hall, Boston, MA, 18 September 2023
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry performed at Brighton Music Hall in Boston, MA on 2023-09-18 (a Monday), as part of her first solo headline tour. [1]
- The solo headline tour was described as sold out. [1]
- ggwendolyn performed as the opening act; Brighton Music Hall was already crowded before her set began. [1]
- The 2023-09-18 Brighton Music Hall concert was Lauren Mayberry's second Boston-area performance within less than a week; she had previously played MGM Music Hall at Fenway on 2023-09-12. [1]
- At the time of the concert, all but one of Mayberry's new solo songs remained unreleased; the only commercially available track was "Are You Awake?" The set was described as notably short as a result. [1]
- The set included at least: "Are You Awake?", covers of Madonna's "Like a Prayer" and 'Til Tuesday's "Voices Carry", and the closing number "Sorry Etc." [1]
- Mayberry delivered her performance with what the reviewer described as "practiced theatricality." [1]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry — Support Slots with Death Cab for Cutie and The Postal Service, US, 2023
Facts:
- As part of her US solo dates preceding the UK/EU tour leg, Lauren Mayberry performed support slots with Death Cab for Cutie and The Postal Service, who were celebrating the 20th anniversary of "Transatlanticism" and "Give Up" respectively. [1]
- Mayberry stated: "This tour offer came through and it seemed too good to be true. Obviously I love both those bands, both those records, and I love Jenny Lewis and Rilo Kiley. I feel like a bit of a creep because I've been lucky enough to be in and around the Death Cab cinematic universe for a while now." [1]
- Mayberry described her support slots with The Postal Service as "quite poetic," noting that The Postal Service were "a band that Chvrches talked about a lot right in the beginning when they were forming." [1]
- Lauren Mayberry played MGM Music Hall at Fenway in Boston on 2023-09-12; this performance is contextually associated with the Death Cab for Cutie / Postal Service support run — a review of her Brighton Music Hall headline show five days later notes it as her immediately preceding Boston appearance, within a passage describing her support slots on that tour. [2]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry — Public Stance on Gender in Music
Facts:
- In a September 2023 backstage interview at her first solo concert, Mayberry described how CHVRCHES had triggered sustained external attention to her gender identity: "I never thought about my gender a huge amount until we were in this band. And it was brought up to me over and over and over and over, and I kind of felt like it's just this thing that's being said at me, but I'm not really creating a huge amount to do with any of these experiences. And I don't feel like I was really using any of that in a productive way. I was kind of keeping it out of the creative and it was being brought at me all the time." [1]
- Shortly after the release of The Bones of What You Believe (2013), Mayberry penned an editorial for The Guardian titled "I will not accept online misogyny," detailing misogynistic, violent, and sexually assaultive language directed at her via the band's social media accounts. After displaying one particularly offensive message on the band's Facebook page, the post received over 1,000 comments, many of them similarly abusive; Mayberry was called a "bitch," a "slut," and was threatened with rape. Mayberry also appeared solo on Channel 4 News to speak directly about the abuse — a rare exception to the band's standard policy of appearing together as a unit. [2,10,14,15]
- Mayberry stated to NME, of her decision to speak out about online abuse: "Someone asked me, 'Do you regret opening this can of worms?' Well, it was opened for me. It was opened at me." [14]
- On a separate occasion (date unspecified, but mentioned as "more recently" as of mid-2015), Mayberry posted an Instagram message from a follower who had written to tell her he was fantasizing about sexually abusing her with a cheese grater. Mayberry's public response: "I am not going anywhere. So bring it on, motherfuckers. Let's see who blinks first." [2]
- The sustained harassment caused Mayberry to pull back from the band's social media accounts, delegating monitoring to other people, and to stop using social media privately altogether. She described her reasoning: "I don't have them because I don't want people to have a direct line to abusing me, to be honest." By the spring of 2015 she had set up her own personal Twitter account, and became proficient at deleting and reporting abusive messages. [2]
- Mayberry reflected in mid-2015 that publicly confronting the issue had, paradoxically, opened a wider dialogue with fans of both genders: "It's been really amazing to see the young guys who have come to us and talked about it... The stereotypes about masculinity for guys are as damaging as the stereotypes about femininity are to women." [2]
- Mayberry stated in mid-2015 that she experienced misogynistic treatment as "the standard" in alternative rock — citing the response of other industry figures as well: "everyone was like, 'Oh, well. It's just a thing that happens'." She quoted an unnamed woman online who told her: "Well, if you don't have thicker skin, you shouldn't be a woman in the music industry" — which Mayberry characterised as "a really warped way of looking at things." [2]
- Lauren Mayberry stated in a 2013 interview that she spent most of her time "trying to be just a person in a rock band rather than singled out as a girl in a rock band," adding "you can only fight the good fight" — an on-the-record statement on gender dynamics in music predating her later Guardian op-ed on the same topic. [3]
- In a 2013 Anchor Shop TV interview during CHVRCHES' first North American tour, Mayberry stated the band's public presentation was deliberately holistic: "It's not two producers with a singer at the front. It's a three-part writing process. And that's how we want it to be. And how we would like it to be perceived." She added that she would not be "pushed to be something that we're not comfortable with," closing with: "So the Playboy cover shoot will have to wait until we're super, super, super desperate." This is among the earliest on-the-record articulations of this position in the CHVRCHES corpus. [4]
- Mayberry stated in October 2023 that from the outset of CHVRCHES' career, gendered attention was immediate and pervasive: "I was 23 when Chvrches started, and from minute one it felt like all anyone wanted to talk about was my gender. A lot of the narrative was around what I looked like, what the guys didn't look like and how that fit into the context." [5]
- Mayberry stated in October 2023 that public discourse about her gender occurred without her agency: "Everyone was having this conversation about me, at me, and I wasn't really involved in it. If you spend most of your formative years in those environments, how do you psychologically stitch that back together to have a more connected experience with yourself, your life and your writing?" [5]
- Mayberry stated in October 2023 that announcing solo work as a woman in a band reliably generates backlash: "For any frontperson in a band, but especially a woman, as soon as you say the words 'solo material' people get very upset with you. I feel like every second sentence is, 'I'm not breaking up the band, guys, however…'" [5]
- Mayberry stated in October 2023 that despite CHVRCHES being publicly associated with feminist discourse, she was consistently the only woman in most professional environments: "So much of the time, people talk about Chvrches and they talk about feminism, but I was still the only one in most of those rooms in most of those experiences." [5]
- Mayberry stated in October 2023 that there are aspects of female experience she does not feel comfortable performing or writing about in a mixed-gender band context: "There are a lot of specific things about the female experience that won't apply to Iain and Martin... sometimes I don't want to sing about certain things or perform in a certain way when I'm on stage with men – if that doesn't sound terrible. They're the nicest men, but sometimes it's not a conversation that I'm comfortable having fully in that context." [5]
- Mayberry stated in April 2024 that her solo material includes things she would not write or perform in CHVRCHES: "There are certain things that I would not write in CHVRCHES. There are certain things I wouldn't feel comfortable performing in CHVRCHES. When it came to this era, I thought these songs would be uncomfortable to play with straight men." [6]
- Mayberry described the overarching creative brief of her solo project as "trying to zoom in on the things that I wouldn't if I wasn't standing next to two guys in a band," and linked it to making sense of the "isolating" experience of being a woman in a male-dominated music industry. [5]
- In a June 2015 Pitchfork interview, Mayberry recounted being told: "You can't make the kind of music you're making and call yourself a feminist." She stated: "The door was slammed on them swiftly after that. Nothing gets my hackles up like being told I can't do something." The alleged incompatibility was making pop music while not playing an instrument — which Mayberry characterised as "horrible, discriminatory stereotype shit." [7]
- In the same June 2015 interview, Mayberry described CHVRCHES' longstanding policy of not singling her out for solo interviews or photos: "Sometimes constantly being on the offensive did feel pretty tiring, but now we're lucky enough to practice what we preach all the time. The band has an identity that people are aware of, and we don't have to fight that fight every single day." [7]
- At the Òran Mór Q&A on 2024-12-08, Mayberry described the music industry as "a pretty lawless place" when asked whether it was better for women than in 2011–2012: "I think we know more about how some things work now, or maybe I think some stuff would be harder to hide now because of like visibility and the internet and things like that. But generally it's a pretty lawless place — like, you know, shit goes down on tour and you don't tell anybody about it and nobody fucking knows about it." She added: "There's no HR, you can't really like, who do you tell about that? There's no like eject button, like you're just in it." [8]
- Martin Doherty stated during Love Is Dead promotional activity (circa 2018): "I think it's healthy that it's in the mainstream. Of course. But she was getting fucking slaughtered five years ago, for saying stuff that's now everywhere I look. Lauren stood up against this kind of behaviour on day one." [9,16]
- Lauren Mayberry, in a Love Is Dead promotional context (circa 2018), described her experience of growing up publicly in the band: "As a woman in any industry but especially as a woman in the entertainment industry, I knew how people talk about you. How they perceive you. Even before the first record came out I could see all these monsters in the trees already. Some of those monsters turned out to be real. Some of them did not." [9,16]
- Martin Doherty stated to The Guardian (quoted in the BBC EOE promotional feature, ca. 2015): "We could have sold 200,000 more albums if we'd hidden Iain and I from view and put Lauren on the cover of every magazine. But there's 100 of those acts and that stuff goes away. We ended up doing it in an indie band style. We broke through via word of mouth. It was about doing it in an honest, right way." [14]
- At Austin City Limits Festival on 2018-10-13, Mayberry called Texas senator Ted Cruz a "cock popsicle" from the stage. [11]
- At Austin City Limits Festival on 2018-10-13, Mayberry dedicated "Leave a Trace" to women survivors of sexual assault and abuse, saying from the stage: "This is for all the ladies who have survived something. Because it is not our job to get walked over by men." [11]
- Mayberry reflected in December 2024 that being the only woman in the CHVRCHES touring party left her carrying the "emotional labour" of keeping the show on the road: "I feel like I twisted myself into a pretzel sometimes to make everyone happy. Then I'd look back and think, 'And were you happy?' Not really, but I was keeping the peace." [12]
- In August 2015, following the release of the "Leave A Trace" music video — in which Mayberry wore a dress — CHVRCHES received a specific wave of misogynistic messages claiming her appearance was "hypocritical" given her stated views on the objectification of women. Mayberry described still feeling "tender" from the attacks as of late September 2015. [13,15]
- Mayberry stated that the harassment was not contingent on her appearance or clothing: "I can hand-on-heart tell you that people said as much misogynist crap when I was wearing shorts and a t-shirt and 95 denier tights as they do when I am in a video wearing a dress. You could be standing there wearing a boiler suit and people are still going to do it because they don't think there should be a woman in that position." [13]
- Mayberry stated that online harassment is a tool of intimidation, not merely casual abuse: "They don't send those messages to you for a giggle. They send them to scare and intimidate you. If I was like, 'It's ok, I'll just walk away,' it doesn't change anything. You're condoning it by being silent." [13]
- Mayberry articulated the distinction between legitimate criticism and gendered abuse: "If you don't like the record, cool. Everyone is entitled to their opinion. But there is a difference between criticising the work and discussing someone's tit size or whether they give oral sex. There is a line, I think." [13]
- Mayberry reflected in 2018 on the long-term emotional toll of the sustained online harassment following her public advocacy: "Looking back on it now, a lot of it was frustrating and it made me really angry and made me feel really lonely... There were definitely points where... Not where I didn't want to do this any more, but I was like, 'If this is what it's going to be like all the time, I don't really know if I want to be that guy forever'." [15]
- Mayberry stated in 2018 that industry gestures around the #MeToo movement lacked substantive follow-through, specifically citing Harvey Weinstein's expulsion from the Academy while Roman Polanski, Woody Allen, and Bill Cosby remained members: "Yes, it's great that you kicked Harvey Weinstein out of the Academy but Roman Polanski's still in there, Woody Allen's still in there, Bill Cosby's still in there. People wear white roses at the Grammy Awards to show they stand with these things - but then you look at the show, and you look at the content of it, and you look at the people in the room, and you're like, 'Oh, nothing has changed'." [15]
- Mayberry stated in 2018 that the #MeToo movement came as a surprise to men but not to women: "I don't know any women who were surprised by Me Too, but a lot of men were because they didn't realise the extent of it." [15]
- In a February 2023 Billboard interview, Mayberry delivered an on-the-record critique of US alternative radio, citing it as a factor in CHVRCHES seeking a new label approach: "alternative radio in America is all f—king men! It's all men!" She also described an early-career playlisting narrative: "there was a time, at the beginning of the band especially, where there was a narrative of, 'Oh, we've just playlisted [another] band with a female vocalist,' even if they sounded completely different than us." [17]
- In a May 2019 interview, Mayberry argued that meaningful change on gender equality in music requires industry insiders — at labels, management companies, and media outlets — to accept personal financial sacrifice: "I think that the way things will change is if people in the industry, in the media, at labels, at management companies, that's where someone somewhere needs to make a decision where they make less money and then things will be different. Because at the moment, it's still the women that are giving things up and women that are sacrificing things. And people say the right things at dinner parties, but when you're actually in a meeting room with somebody, they can say something completely different." She added: "I don't think there have been new changes. I think there's been a real change in the way that we talk about it." [18]
- At the All Things Go Fall Classic (Union Market Dock 5, Washington, DC, 2019-10-12), Mayberry remarked from the stage: "actually, if I was a rock band guy I could try 40% less hard...I could just roll out of bed and they'd be like, 'genius!'" [19]
- In September 2015, at a Central Park show in New York, Mayberry confronted a male fan who had been shouting "Marry me!" at her from the audience; after subsequent Twitter abuse, she clarified in a separate interview: "All I'm asking for is for my position as a musician and as an artist to be taken as seriously as that of my bandmates, and not to be defined and separated by my gender." [20]
- Iain Cook stated in 2015-10 on the burden of sustained gender-based harassment directed at Mayberry: "Sometimes it rears its ugly head, and we can't not look at it. It's an enormously stressful thing when that happens. I don't know how Lauren has the strength to shoulder all that stuff because it's so personally directed at her. The best thing we can do as bandmates and friends is support her and give her space." [20]
- The Guardian open letter Mayberry wrote in 2013 following the release of The Bones of What You Believe included the statement: "Objectification, whatever its form, is not something anyone should have to 'just deal with'." [21]
- Mayberry stated in August 2021 that gender and "feminism" had always been part of the public conversation around CHVRCHES, but that she had never written about it lyrically: "I've never written about it lyrically... maybe a form of self-defence." [21]
- By August 2021, Mayberry had made a deliberate decision to stop publicly engaging with online conflict: "I knew I needed to not engage with the world in the same way anymore. I don't want to play the victim card, straight white women's tears are not anything we need to be worrying about right now, but it is sad." [21]
- Mayberry stated in August 2021 that every time the band spoke up about rape threats, misogyny, the Marshmello/Chris Brown statement, or Hatebreed's vocalist Jamey Jasta's objection to their billing at Deftones' Día De Los festival, the band received further abuse: "they've received even more abuse back." [21]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry, SXSW 2019 Keynote Speaker in Conversation with Shirley Manson
Facts: - Lauren Mayberry was scheduled as a keynote speaker at SXSW 2019, appearing in conversation with Garbage's Shirley Manson on 2019-03-14 (Thursday). [1]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry opening at The Anthem, Washington, D.C., 6 September 2023
Facts: - The article reports that Lauren Mayberry opened for The Postal Service and Death Cab for Cutie at The Anthem on 2023-09-06. [1]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry — UK and European Solo Tour, October 2023
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry launched the UK and European leg of her first solo headline tour in her native Glasgow on approximately 5 October 2023; remaining dates were: 6 Oct – Birmingham O2 Academy 2; 8 Oct – Manchester Academy 2; 9 Oct – London KOKO; 11 Oct – Amsterdam Melkweg Oz; 12 Oct – Berlin Lido; 13 Oct – Munich Strom; 15 Oct – Paris La Maroquinerie; 16 Oct – Cologne Luxor; 17 Oct – Prague Rock Cafe; 19 Oct – Vienna Flex; 21 Oct – Zurich Bogen F; 22 Oct – Milan Magnolia; 24 Oct – Barcelona La Nau; 25 Oct – Madrid Sala Copernico. [1]
- At each date on the tour, Mayberry delivered a city-specific special cover version; the covers were not disclosed in advance and were often prepared during the hour or so she and her band had together at soundcheck. [1,2]
- Mayberry described her mood at the outset of the solo project as "the usual new music anxiety multiplied by, 'What if nobody wants this or if everyone thinks you're a fucking idiot for wanting to do it' anxiety." [1]
- Mayberry stated she had felt encouraged by the crowd's reaction to new material at the US dates preceding the UK/EU leg. [1]
- During the UK leg, Mayberry appeared on BBC Radio 1's "Hottest Record in the World" segment on 2023-10-10 to promote the solo single "Shame." [3]

Caption: "Leave a Trace" (Song)
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry described "Leave a Trace" as "the nastiest, snidest tune" on Every Open Eye and characterised it as "the middle finger mic-drop" — a song about a relationship that has reached a point where no resolution is possible: "There's no point having this conversation anyways: There will be no resolve, I won't feel better about it, you won't feel better about it, no outcome from this will actually change my reality." [1]
- Pitchfork's Laura Snapes describes "Leave a Trace" as "R&B-inflected"; Mayberry's vocal performance on the track is characterised as "deeper and more soulful than ever." The lyric includes a portrait of a lover who talks "far too much for someone so unkind." [1]
- In August 2015, the release of the "Leave A Trace" music video — in which Mayberry wore a dress — triggered a specific wave of misogynistic messages, with individuals claiming her appearance was "hypocritical" given her publicly stated views on the objectification of women. Mayberry was called "a slut," "a whore," and "a bitch." She replied publicly, calling the anonymous writers "cavemen" and describing their threats as "sadly predictable." [2]
- As of the BBC interview published 2015-09-26, the "Leave A Trace" video had accumulated more than 2.2 million views. [2]
- "Leave A Trace" was released as the lead single from Every Open Eye on 2015-09-18 (global / US), one week before the parent album; the Australian commercial single release predated this at 2015-07-20 (per australian-charts.com). [3,2]

Caption: CHVRCHES Post "Lies" Online, May 2012
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- In May 2012, CHVRCHES anonymously posted their first song, "Lies," online. [1,2]
- "Lies" quickly became a viral hit and received airplay on BBC Radio 1. [1]
- "Lies" first generated significant interest in the Glasgow music press before spreading nationally, including coverage in the NME. [3]
- "Lies" attracted the attention of The Guardian in 2012-05 and was described by a BBC feature as the band's "first download release." [5]
- "Lies" was later released in Australia as an official Vertigo commercial single on 2013-12-02, per the australian-charts.com singles discography — a post-album promotional single release following the September 2013 release of The Bones of What You Believe. [4]
- BBC Radio One Introducing presenter Ally McCrae stated he was the first radio presenter to play "Lies" on air, having done so on his BBC Radio One Introducing show. [6]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Live Out Festival, Monterrey — 1 October 2016
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at the Live Out Festival in Monterrey, Mexico on 2016-10-01; this was the second edition of the Live Out Festival. [1]
- The CHVRCHES set at Live Out Festival Monterrey was shorter than their set at El Plaza Condesa, CDMX three days earlier on 2016-09-28. [1]

Caption: Love Is Dead — Japan Deluxe Edition (HSE-6864, September 2018)
Facts:
- The Love Is Dead Japan Deluxe Edition was released on September 14, 2018 by Hostess Entertainment (catalog number HSE-6864), priced at ¥2,500+tax; it was labelled a Japan-exclusive special edition (日本独自企画盤). [1]
- The Japan Deluxe Edition contains the standard 13-track Love Is Dead plus five Japan-exclusive tracks: "Out Of My Head feat. 水曜日のカンパネラ" (track 14, a new collaborative song) and four asterisked bonus tracks — "Get Out (Roosevelt Remix)," "Miracle (IHF Remix)," "Miracle (Mija Remix)," and "Miracle (The Juan Maclean Remix)" (tracks 15–18). The product page header describes the edition as containing "4 bonus tracks" (ボーナストラック4曲), which refers only to the asterisked remix tracks; "Out Of My Head" is presented as a separate Japan-exclusive addition beyond the bonus track count. [1]
- The Japan Deluxe Edition includes a Japanese lyrics translation (歌詞対訳) and liner notes written by Ito Natsumi (伊藤なつみ). [1]

Caption: Love Is Dead — Recording Sessions
Facts:
- CHVRCHES began writing Love Is Dead at a project space in Bushwick, Brooklyn, in 2017-01. Lauren Mayberry confirmed in a May 2018 interview, conducted before the album's release: "we started writing in a little project space in Bushwick in like January last year." She had relocated to New York between tours towards the end of the Every Open Eye cycle prior to those sessions. [1]
- By February 2018, the band had been "holed up for a year" working on the album. Martin Doherty: "We've been like kind of holed up for a year now, trying to make this album." [2]
- Love Is Dead was the first CHVRCHES album made with an outside producer. Lauren Mayberry stated in February 2018: "the previous two records had just been these two guys and we made them in Glasgow and this time we worked with Greg Kurstin." Doherty described their historical wariness of collaboration — with 'Certainly not' being their habitual response to proposals — explaining the reasoning: "We knew that if you get led down the wrong path it can completely derail your career." [2,6]
- Lauren Mayberry described Greg Kurstin's production range as central to his appeal: "He can do an Adele record and he can do a Beck record or a Foo Fighters record and there's no like one sound that he puts on stuff... he has this understanding of the pop world but he also has done so much alternative music. And I feel like that's great because we sit in the middle of those two things." [2]
- Lauren Mayberry described Love Is Dead as being about "growing up" and "coming to terms with the fact that there are great things in the world and there are awful things in the world and that you can't get one without the other." [9]
- Lauren Mayberry described Greg Kurstin's brief to the band during the Love Is Dead sessions: "how do we do what you're doing? But push yourselves." [2]
- CHVRCHES described Greg Kurstin's production approach during the Love Is Dead sessions: "He doesn't try to make you write a certain kind of song. He just listens and then Jedi puppet masters the best work out of you." [3]
- For Love Is Dead, all three band members spent the entire creative process together in one room — a departure from earlier practice in which Mayberry would disappear for days at a time to write lyrics alone. Mayberry described the result: "feels a lot more coherent." [4,6]
- Lauren Mayberry described her lyric focus on Love Is Dead: "I tried to write less about romantic love specifically. And more about the overarching concept of love." [4,6]
- Lauren Mayberry described the societal context that informed Love Is Dead: "We live in a time where the death of empathy is pretty evident. You turn on the news and see story after story about how selfish and unkind people are, but also stories of strength and resilience and resistance. How do we deal with both those things?" [4,6]
- Greg Kurstin recalled how working with CHVRCHES came together quickly for Love Is Dead: "We had similar musical tastes and we clicked in the writing process pretty quickly. I got to pull out all of the things I don't have a chance to normally use." [4,6]
- Martin Doherty on Love Is Dead: "It makes more sense than anything else we've made because it most accurately represents us. We've always had this super difficult side to us – artistic and introspective and angry, but then we also fully embrace commercial music. When we talked about this album, before we even made it, we talked about it that way, we talked about there being two sides to the band. With Love Is Dead, we've broadened the appeal, but there are moments that are more difficult than anything we've ever done." [4,6]
- Iain Cook, after CHVRCHES' first two albums, felt the band had built "a protective armour around that core" — sufficient creative identity to safely invite outside collaboration for Love Is Dead. Doherty paraphrased Cook's position: "Cook felt they'd done enough to build 'a protective armour around that core'. The time had come to see what other writers and producers could bring to the band." Doherty further noted the band's earlier stance: "We were incredibly against any idea of outside collaboration whilst we were forming our identity." [5]
- An early Love Is Dead collaboration attempt with unnamed "well-known producers" yielded no usable material; the producers added a chorus to a track and immediately departed, leaving the song undeveloped. Mayberry: "A couple of well-known producers came in and kind of sprinkled a chorus on, and then they just left... 'Boof! There's your chorus. Goodbye'. That song did not make it any further." [5]
- Other early Love Is Dead collaborators pushed CHVRCHES toward a commercial "top 40" direction, which Doherty stated was "never us, really": "They were like, 'Well, it's top 40 time, isn't it? Let's go!'" [5]
- CHVRCHES worked on Love Is Dead with Eurythmics co-founder Dave Stewart; none of the songs he produced for the band made the final album. Mayberry confirmed in a May 2019 interview: "We were really bummed that the songs we did didn't make the album. But they just felt quite removed from everything else by the time we'd finished, because that was right at the start of the process." Despite this, his unconventional techniques had a significant creative impact. Doherty described Stewart as a formative influence: "Talk about going to the source. I don't want to say we've ripped him off, but I'm extremely influenced by him." Mayberry described his mentorship as having "really pushed us to focus on the artistic integrity of the album and everything that surrounds it." [5,6,8]
- Dave Stewart maintained a daily recording ritual he called "Martini hour." [5]
- During a Love Is Dead recording session, Dave Stewart challenged Mayberry to fully inhabit the "punk rock Joan of Arc of pop music" persona he perceived in her: "You know, I think that you want to be this punk rock Joan of Arc of pop music - so why don't you just stop complaining and do it already?" Mayberry recalled being "shocked and mildly offended at first" but later reflected during a taxi ride home: "I don't know if I feel like that, but if he can see it in me, I should probably think about it." [5]
- Martin Doherty observed that Stewart's challenge "definitely unlocked something in [Mayberry] lyrically," crediting him with igniting "a fire and help[ing] us find a type of creative ambition that was maybe missing in the past." [5]
- Greg Kurstin's studio, where Love Is Dead was recorded, was described by Doherty and Mayberry as a "dingy basement" of "wall-to-wall keyboards" with a constant "buzz and hum of electronics." [5]
- Love Is Dead was recorded across three cities — New York, London, and Los Angeles. The writing process began at a project space in Bushwick, Brooklyn (New York); sessions with Greg Kurstin took place in Los Angeles. Within one week of starting Kurstin sessions, CHVRCHES had completed four songs; impressed with the results, they cancelled all other planned sessions to work exclusively with him. [5,7]
- Lauren Mayberry's lead vocals for Love Is Dead were recorded by Greg Kurstin on the same microphone he had recently used to record Adele, Dave Grohl, and Paul McCartney. [5]
- Greg Kurstin had won five Grammy Awards as of the Love Is Dead recording period, per a BBC journalist's annotation (May 2018). He was described at the time as "the producer behind Sia's Chandelier and co-writer of Adele's Hello." Mayberry characterised Kurstin's studio demeanour as unpretentious: "He doesn't come in thinking, 'I'm this guy who made this record and I have this many Grammys'." [5]
- Martin Doherty described the band's US relocation as intensifying the introspective, world-weary tone of Love Is Dead: "It made us go in on ourselves even more, being in that somewhat hostile atmosphere." [5]
- Iain Cook summarized the album's ethos during LID promo: "We're fucked, the world is fucked. But there's an ellipsis at the end. It's Love Is Dead…. Like, how did we get to this point? And how do we move on from this point? It's Love Is Dead, we're fucked, what's next?" [6]
- CHVRCHES stopped touring towards the end of 2016; before beginning Love Is Dead sessions in early 2017, Lauren Mayberry took a holiday to an island in the Bahamas "solely populated by iguanas" — the first break between the end of the Every Open Eye touring cycle and the start of writing the new album. [7]
- Over 40 songs were written in total during the Love Is Dead sessions; Doherty described having enough unheard demo material for "a proper full-on guitar album." [7]
