- Three tracks on Love Is Dead were self-produced by CHVRCHES (not produced with Greg Kurstin): "My Enemy" (feat. Matt Berninger of The National), "God's Plan," and "Really Gone." After six months of writing and recording, Doherty said the band were able to see these three tracks "through to the end of the process." On the decision to self-produce, Doherty quipped: "It was cheap." [7]
- Mayberry stated on the record about Kurstin's approach to songwriting: "These are still CHVRCHES' songs — he would never even step in on the toplines unless we had all hit a wall." She also described his keyboard playing as inspiring, saying she "genuinely felt like he was coaxing the best melodies out of everybody, out of me, without saying a word." [7]

Caption: Love Is Dead (Album Release)
Facts:
- Love Is Dead is CHVRCHES' third studio album. [1,2]
- Love Is Dead was released in Australia on 2018-05-25 on the Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol multi-label imprint. It entered the Australian album chart on 2018-06-10, peaked at #7, and spent 3 weeks on the chart. [3]
- Dave Stewart served as CHVRCHES' mentor during the making of Love Is Dead. [2]
- Love Is Dead is CHVRCHES' first album to feature outside production. Lauren Mayberry confirmed in February 2018: "the previous two records had just been these two guys and we made them in Glasgow and this time we worked with Greg Kurstin." [4,5]
- Greg Kurstin produced nine of Love Is Dead's tracks; his other production credits include Adele, Kelly Clarkson, P!nk, Sia, and Foo Fighters. [2,6,4]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: Kurstin track count — both the LKA Longhorn Stuttgart promotional text (translated from German) [7] and the Beat Yard festival bio (English) [13] state Kurstin co-produced eight of twelve album tracks, conflicting with the nine-track figure on a thirteen-track album cited by Billboard and AV Club.
- Steve Mac co-produced "Miracle" on Love Is Dead; his other production credits include Ed Sheeran, Susan Boyle, and Westlife. [4,13]
- In 2017, CHVRCHES went into the studio with producer Greg Kurstin and collaborator Dave Stewart to work on Love Is Dead. [8]
- Love Is Dead debuted at number seven in the U.K. [8,9]
- Love Is Dead debuted at #1 on Billboard's Top Rock Albums and Alternative Albums charts (both dated June 9, 2018), with 28,000 equivalent units — of which 23,000 were traditional album sales; it was CHVRCHES' second No. 1 on both charts. The Alternative Albums chart run lasted 2 weeks. [8,10,4]
- Love Is Dead debuted at #11 on the all-genre Billboard 200. [4,9]
- Chris Gehringer served as mastering engineer for Love Is Dead; the Hostess product page cites his mastering credits as including Rihanna and Massive Attack. [9]
- Spike Stent served as mixing engineer for Love Is Dead; the Hostess product page cites his mixing credits as including U2, Madonna, and Björk. [9]
- Greg Kurstin had won the Grammy Award for Producer of the Year in two consecutive years as of the time of Love Is Dead's release (2018); the Hostess product page also cites his credits as including Adele and Beck alongside this Grammy recognition. [9]
- CHVRCHES described their experience working with Greg Kurstin on Love Is Dead: "Working with Greg was so different to what we'd done before, but it also felt so comfortable and like he'd been in our band forever." The quote was originally sourced from Entertainment Weekly and was also rendered in Japanese in the Hostess product notes. [9,11]
- Lauren Mayberry described Love Is Dead to Entertainment Weekly: "When I listen to the record, I feel like it's the most pop stuff we've done and also the most aggressive and vulnerable at the same time. It was about really leaning into those moments—and also wanting there to be a real humanity and character to what we're saying." The same sentiment was rendered in Japanese in the Hostess product notes as "これまでで最もポップな作品" ("our most pop work"). [9,11]
- As of 2015-10, while actively promoting Every Open Eye, CHVRCHES were already generating ideas for what became Love Is Dead using portable recording rigs on the road. Doherty described road-writing as therapeutic: "When you've been on tour for two years and you haven't flexed a creative muscle for months, then you just feel like a promo machine that plays gigs." His road rig consisted of Cubase on a Mac with software synthesizers; Cook used a Prophet 12 module. [12]
- Love Is Dead debuted and peaked at #22 on the Billboard Canadian Albums chart (chart week 2018-06-09), spending 1 week on the chart. [14]
- Mayberry explained the thinking behind the album title "Love Is Dead" in a release-day Billboard interview (2018-05-25): it was chosen to be "slightly more theatrical" than a lyric from a song. She described it as "a little jarring" but purposeful: "It's supposed to be a starting point for the conversation that happens across the record. Somebody is trying to figure out why they feel the way they do and you don't necessarily have an answer, but you're trying to wade through that frustration and confusion — and what do you do with all that once you know." [15]
- Love Is Dead was certified Silver in the UK by the BPI on 2022-08-05; no Gold or higher certification has been issued as of the BPI data. [16]
- Love Is Dead was released via Glassnote Records (US), confirmed in the 2018-02-28 album announcement. [17]
- The full Love Is Dead tracklist in order: (1) Graffiti / (2) Get Out / (3) Deliverance / (4) My Enemy / (5) Forever / (6) Never Say Die / (7) Miracle / (8) Graves / (9) Heaven/Hell / (10) God's Plan / (11) Really Gone / (12) ii / (13) Wonderland. [17]

Caption: CHVRCHES Love Is Dead Tour, 2018
Facts:
- The Love Is Dead live touring lineup comprised Lauren Mayberry, Martin Doherty, and Iain Cook, supplemented by touring drummer Jonny Scott; Scott is named explicitly in the Stuttgart review (11 November 2018) and the Amnplify Sydney review (22 July 2018), while the addition of a live drummer was first noted as new at the Sydney Australian shows in July 2018. [1,2,3,4,8]
- The Love Is Dead tour was the first CHVRCHES tour on which the band had a dedicated monitor engineer. Previously, audio routing had been handled through the computer with the band manually muting excess microphones to manage stage sound. Iain Cook confirmed: "we've got a monitor engineer now. We didn't have one before. He's hooked up so we can actually all hear each other in between songs." Martin Doherty added: "I haven't heard Iain on stage in any capacity for about five years." Confirmed as new as of the opening Elsewhere, Brooklyn shows in May 2018. [5]
- Martin Doherty consistently took lead vocal on "God's Plan" during the Love Is Dead tour, moving to the front of the stage mid-set, with Lauren Mayberry in a supporting role; confirmed at Sydney (22 July 2018), Melbourne (23 July 2018), Denver (7 August 2018), and Stuttgart (11 November 2018). [1,2,3,6]
- Martin Doherty also performed "Under the Tide" from the front of stage mid-set on the Love Is Dead tour; explicitly confirmed at Stuttgart (11 November 2018), and the song appears in the setlists from Melbourne (23 July 2018) and Denver (7 August 2018). [2,3,6]
- The 2019 UK headline dates of the Love Is Dead tour were described as CHVRCHES' first UK headline tour since November 2015. [7]
- During the 2019 Australian leg, CHVRCHES were given a four-day break in Melbourne. Mayberry had expected to spend the downtime with her bandmates and the touring crew, but discovered they had each made separate plans, leaving her alone in her hotel room. [9]
- Left alone in Melbourne, Mayberry hired a small car and drove to an Australian spa town, where she had "a wee cry" listening to Taylor Swift's "Cruel Summer." [9]
- Mayberry described the Melbourne incident as a "humbling moment": "I remember being very upset and hurt by that because I was always worrying about everyone else and taking care of everybody, and it was a humbling moment." [9]
- Mayberry considered leaving CHVRCHES after the Melbourne incident; the COVID-19 pandemic then intervened, and the band instead made Screen Violence (2021) remotely rather than Mayberry departing at that point. [9]
- Looking back on the Melbourne incident, Mayberry attributed the dynamic to being the only woman in the touring party, reflecting that she had been left carrying the "emotional labour" of keeping the show on the road. [9]
- CHVRCHES officially announced the end of the Love Is Dead touring cycle via their Tumblr, writing: "And that's a wrap on the touring for Love Is Dead. Thank you to everyone who was there with us. Can't wait to see you on the next one." The post was published in approximately late 2019, consistent with the Intersect Music Festival (December 2019) being among the final confirmed tour dates. [10]
- After completing the final US leg of the Love Is Dead tour, the band took a three-month break before beginning work on Screen Violence; Mayberry described it as "the longest [break] since the band had formed in 2011." [11]

Caption: "Mantra" — Lauren Mayberry Solo Track
Facts:
- "Mantra" originated from a conversation between Mayberry and a friend about dating "really shit terrible boys in bands"; the underlying question was whether the attraction was to the people themselves or to the idea of being like them — the "do you want to be with them or do you want to be them" dynamic. [1]
- Mayberry described writing the song from a "witchy hexing angle" — the perspective of someone asserting power over another person. [1]
- Mayberry identified the lyric "with a voice that never could quite command respect" as particularly meaningful to her personally, relating to her experience of opinions that she felt had not been accorded the respect they deserved. [1]
- Mayberry characterised "Mantra" as part of a broader pattern in the Vicious Creature album: writing about a power or persona she wished she had — "here's the things I would say if I wasn't such a sort of afraid of you." [1]

Caption: Martin Doherty — BBC Radio 2 and Boiler Room Double-Session Day (TBOWYTB Era)
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- Martin Doherty cited performing a BBC Radio 2 session in the afternoon and a Boiler Room session that same evening as "probably one of my proudest achievements" — representing CHVRCHES' ability to bridge a mainstream radio audience and a more discerning electronic music community on a single day. Doherty: "I remember one day when I realized that our band had the chance of achieving what we'd set out to do: We did a BBC Radio 2 session in the afternoon and a Boiler Room one that night. That was probably one of my proudest achievements — the idea that you can make music that people can stick on the radio that can also be relevant and interesting to people who are more discerning." This event occurred in the TBOWYTB touring period; its date was unknown in the June 2015 Pitchfork article. [1]
- The BBC Radio 2 session and Boiler Room set both occurred on the same day they released The Bones of What You Believe, per Doherty: "The day that we put out Bones, I knew we were doing something I believed in wholly because we did a Radio 2 session — which is like a BBC station for old people — and a Boiler Room set on the same day." This places the event on approximately 2013-09-23 (UK release date) or 2013-09-24 (US/international release date). [2]
- ⚠️ Conflicts with: the Boiler Room Album Launch Party listing confirms CHVRCHES played Boiler Room on 2013-09-27 — four days after the UK album release date of 2013-09-23 — suggesting either that Doherty used "the day we put out Bones" loosely to mean the album launch period (and the BBC Radio 2 session also occurred on 2013-09-27), or that a separate, unverified Boiler Room streaming set occurred on the UK release date. — [3], fact "The day that we put out Bones...we did a Radio 2 session...and a Boiler Room set on the same day."

Caption: Martin Doherty — Biographical Facts
Facts:
- Martin Doherty was born on 1982-12-24.
- Martin Doherty named Emma Watson as his celebrity crush in a 2013 interview with 99Scenes. [1]
- Martin Doherty studied music at the University of Strathclyde. He confirmed this during a 2016 Coachella interview: "I was studying music at university." [2]
- Martin Doherty described LCD Soundsystem as "one of my favourite bands," as contextualised during a 2016 Coachella interview when he mentioned leaving their set early the night before to be prepared for the CHVRCHES main stage performance. [2]
- Martin Doherty grew up in Erskine, near Paisley, on the West side of Glasgow near the Erskine Bridge — known locally as the "suicide bridge." Doherty described it: "A lot of dark stuff happens off that bridge. Every night you sit in your house and you hear a helicopter, you're like, 'Somebody else has jumped off.'" [3,4]
- Martin Doherty studied at Glasgow's University of Strathclyde, where he met Iain Cook in 2003; Cook was lecturing a class at Strathclyde in which Doherty was a student, and Cook first noticed Doherty for the shirt he was wearing. [5,4,6]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: the shirt — AllMusic and Consequence of Sound describe it as a "Paul Harvey t-shirt," while Stereogum (2023) identifies it as a "PJ Harvey shirt." PJ Harvey (the musician) is the more contextually plausible referent. [5,4,6]
- Doherty described his state of mind in the period just before CHVRCHES formed as one of anxiety and failure: "I was a very anxious person who considered himself a failure, and was very eager to prove to the world I had something to offer as a creator." He stated he was "starting to think that time was almost up for me" as a musician; in a separate 2018 interview he said directly: "I was at the point where I was about to quit the music business, because I felt like a failure and I had no prospects. Then we wrote those songs and they were coming out quite fully formed and I thought, 'Oh, maybe we can do this.'" [6,13]
- Martin Doherty's role in Aereogramme and The Twilight Sad was as live keyboardist and guitarist. [7]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: Paste Magazine (March 2013) characterises Doherty's contribution to Aereogramme as "engineer and contributing musician" rather than live keyboardist and guitarist — the two characterisations may describe different aspects of his involvement. [8]
- Martin Doherty occasionally DJs in Glasgow under the alias "Digital W.I.N.C.H.," playing obscure electronic music. [7]
- On tour, Doherty took to road-recording as a coping mechanism to maintain his sense of musical identity. During the TBOWYTB campaign the Pitchfork journalist described it as recording one song or idea daily "to make him at least feel like a musician." Doherty elaborated in a 2015-10 Sound on Sound interview: "When you've been on tour for two years and you haven't flexed a creative muscle for months, then you just feel like a promo machine that plays gigs. I use it as a means of therapy over anything else." His road rig was Cubase running on a Mac with software synthesizers only. [9,10]
- In his pre-CHVRCHES bands, Doherty had always been a performer rather than a writer. Doherty: "In those bands I'd played in, I'd never been a writer. I'd always just kind of watched and listened and wrote in my spare time." CHVRCHES was the first band in which he brought his private writing into an equal creative partnership. [10]
- Doherty articulated his creative shift in the EOE era: "I spent half my 20s trying to make weird music immediate, like, 'Why can't I make a post-rock record that sounds like Whitney Houston?' But now we're trying to make immediate music a little bit weirder." [11]
- As a teenager, Doherty listened to Steve Lamacq and John Peel on BBC Radio; he described having a Maida Vale session as "kind of a goal for me as a teenager." [12]
- Approximately one year before April 2013 (~2012), Doherty wrote a fan letter to Charles Bernstein, the composer of the A Nightmare on Elm Street score; Bernstein replied: "I hope you make it and thank you very much." Doherty described the reply as "a real highlight." [14]
- Doherty cited Brad Fiedel (composer of the Terminator score) as among the 1980s film composers who influenced CHVRCHES; Cook was about to name Fiedel before Doherty preempted him, asking: "Are you going to talk about Brad Fiedel?" [14]
- On his music education: "What I took from studying music was what not to do as opposed to what to do. I was around a lot of people who were exceptionally gifted in terms of their technicality who didn't know when to shut up." Cook added: "When not to play." (April 2013 interview.) [14]
- Doherty has struggled with anxiety and depression since he was a teenager; during the Love Is Dead US tour in 2019, he coped by spending time "hiding in the most remote corner of some venue, making music on my laptop." [15]

Caption: Martin Doherty in The Twilight Sad's Live Lineup
Facts:
- After leaving Aereogramme by 2007, Martin Doherty joined The Twilight Sad as a touring member, serving as live keyboardist and guitarist; he stayed with the band for almost five years, which he described as "the best times of his life." [1,2,3,4,5]
- Doherty specifically cited The Twilight Sad's 2009 American tour with Frightened Rabbit and We Were Promised Jetpacks as an especially memorable period: "It was some of the most debauched times that I'd ever seen on the road. A lot of heavy drinking. Don't put 15 Scottish guys on a tour bus together to travel around and play gigs." [5]
- In a 2013 Anchor Shop TV interview during CHVRCHES' first North American tour, Doherty confirmed multiple prior North American tours in his pre-CHVRCHES career: "I think after your fourth time around North America in the back of a van, I defy anyone that says it doesn't change the perception of the music industry on a level. And I've done that a lot in a previous band." The "previous band" is not named but contextually refers to The Twilight Sad, the only pre-CHVRCHES band documented as having toured North America with Doherty. [6]
- Doherty stated that he ultimately left The Twilight Sad because playing someone else's songs felt like "taking the easy way out" and being "on someone else's ticket": "Those guys will always be my best friends. But sooner or later, you come to realize that when you're playing someone else's songs, however amazing they are, there's always something in the back of your mind saying, 'You're taking the easy way out.'" [5]

Caption: "Miracle" (Song)
Facts:
- "Miracle" was released as a single on 2018-04-10; the official CHVRCHES Instagram account posted "MIRACLE is out now" on that date. [7]
- "Miracle" was described as "the latest single" off Love Is Dead during CHVRCHES' July 2018 Australian tour dates. [1]
- "Miracle" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2018-06-08 on the Goodbye / Vertigo / Capitol imprint, per the australian-charts.com singles discography. [2]
- "Miracle" peaked at #33 on the Hot Rock Songs chart (a new peak as of the June 9, 2018 chart); it was also "bubbling under" the Alternative Songs airplay chart at the same date. [3]
- Three remixes of "Miracle" were included as Japan-exclusive bonus tracks on the Love Is Dead Japan Deluxe Edition (HSE-6864, Hostess Entertainment, September 2018): the IHF Remix, the Mija Remix, and The Juan Maclean Remix. [4]
- "Miracle" was referred to as a single ("the pre-apocalyptic single Miracle") in a BBC profile published on 2018-05-01, before Love Is Dead's 2018-05-25 release, confirming its pre-release single status. [5]
- Steve Mac co-produced "Miracle" on Love Is Dead. [6]

Caption: "My Enemy" (Song)
Facts:
- "My Enemy" is a duet between CHVRCHES and Matt Berninger of the National on Love Is Dead. [1,2]
- "My Enemy" was self-produced by CHVRCHES — one of three tracks on Love Is Dead not produced with Greg Kurstin; the other two self-produced tracks were "God's Plan" and "Really Gone." [5]
- "My Enemy" peaked at #28 on the Hot Rock Songs chart. [3]
- Live during the Love Is Dead tour, Martin Doherty covered the Matt Berninger studio part on "My Enemy," sharing vocal duties with Lauren Mayberry; confirmed at the Hordern Pavilion, Sydney on 2018-07-22 (the article refers to the song as "The Enemy"). [4]
- "My Enemy" was debuted publicly as a newly revealed single on 2018-02-28, shared alongside the Love Is Dead album announcement. [6]

Caption: Neon Gold Records Posts "Lies" — CHVRCHES' First Public Single, May 2012
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- Neon Gold Records posted CHVRCHES' first single, "Lies," on 2012-05-11; this was the band's first public-facing music release under the CHVRCHES name. At the time of the upload, CHVRCHES had only six songs total — Cook: "We didn't really have that many songs when we put 'Lies' out. Our whole oeuvre was like six songs at that point." [1,2,3]
- After posting on 2012-05-11, "Lies" climbed to the number one position on The Hype Machine, an MP3 aggregator blog; the response on SoundCloud was also described as enthusiastic. [3]
- At the time "Lies" was posted by Neon Gold in May 2012, CHVRCHES had never played a live show. Martin Doherty stated: "When we put 'Lies' out we'd never played a gig... at that point we hadn't even considered playing a gig. We were pretty far behind in the plot of what had to be done in order to perform." [1]
- The decision to release anonymously was also to avoid local-scene associations — Mayberry: "We were thinking more about [people in the local scene] than anything outside of that — so people in the local scene wouldn't go, 'Oh, it's that guy from Aereogramme, and that guy from Julia Thirteen, and that girl from Blue Sky Archives.'" Doherty added that the band were "totally unsigned at that point, so there was no real plan other than us discovering that if you put out a song online that blows up, the next time you put out a song, more people will listen to it." [2]
- The very first music Cook and Doherty ever made together was witch house — they had considered becoming a witch house act with song titles as symbols before pivoting to what became CHVRCHES: "The idea of confusing people and telling people all the lies you could about who you were and what you were doing was something I saw as a big bonus." (Doherty) [2]
- Neon Gold had initially asked CHVRCHES to write a biographical blog about themselves before posting the single, which the band was unable to write. Martin Doherty recalled: "Neon Gold asked us to write a blog about what the band was and we were just like 'I don't know.'" [1]
- After the upload, "Lies" spread entirely through peer-to-peer sharing with no promotional push behind it. Martin Doherty stated: "No one expected the reaction that the music got from people passing it around to their friends. It was all very organic and steamrolled very quickly and people kept asking 'a show? A show?'" [1]
- The Neon Gold premiere post header reads "THURSDAY, MAY 10, 2012" with a timestamp of "12:00 AM," establishing 2012-05-10 as the publication date per the primary source document. [4]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: the first fact in this event, which dates the post to "2012-05-11" — that date is sourced from Under the Radar and Paste Magazine retrospective accounts; the blog post itself is the primary document and gives 2012-05-10.
- At the time of the Neon Gold premiere post, the band name was spelled "CHURCHES" throughout — the distinctive "v" orthography (CHVRCHES) had not yet been publicised or adopted at the moment of their first public introduction. [4]
- The "Lies" MP3 was labeled "[exclusive]" in the Neon Gold premiere post, indicating Neon Gold had exclusive rights to premiere the track. [4]
- The only biographical description of the band provided in the Neon Gold premiere post was: "CHURCHES are two boys and a girl from Glasgow, and that's about all we know at this juncture" — representing the complete public-facing information about the band at the moment of their debut. [4]
- Lauren Mayberry described the Neon Gold upload as "a preview track or something, just free download, not a release" put out "to kind of test the water and see what people were thinking about it." [5]
- Neon Gold described CHVRCHES as "a godless hurricane of kinetic pop energy" in the "Lies" premiere post — a characterisation subsequently reproduced in CHVRCHES promotional materials. [4]

Caption: "Never Ending Circles" (Song)
Facts:
- "Never Ending Circles" is a track on Every Open Eye; it is built around "a bright, repeatedly stumbling synth part" and contains a middle-eight that Pitchfork's Laura Snapes described as evoking "the more sparkling moments from Taylor Swift's 1989." [1]
- "Never Ending Circles" emerged on the very first day of writing for Every Open Eye. Martin Doherty described it as designed to be a bridge, both sonically and thematically, from The Bones of What You Believe. [2]
- "Never Ending Circles" was the second single from Every Open Eye and served as the standard concert opener for CHVRCHES' Every Open Eye touring cycle; it opened the show at El Plaza Condesa, Mexico City on 2016-09-28. [3]
- The SXSW 2016 official CHVRCHES artist bio characterises "Never Ending Circles" (alongside "Bury It" and "Playing Dead") as being about "being unapologetic and not being told what to do or who to be." [4]

Caption: "Never Say Die" (Song)
Facts:
- "Never Say Die" peaked at #41 on the Hot Rock Songs chart. [1]
- "Never Say Die" was performed in the encore at Sydney Opera House on 2019-01-07, alongside "The Mother We Share." [2]

Caption: NME Awards 2022 — CHVRCHES and Robert Smith Performance
Facts:
- At the NME Awards 2022, CHVRCHES and Robert Smith performed both "How Not To Drown" and "Just Like Heaven" (a song by The Cure) together. [1]
- CHVRCHES and Robert Smith jointly won the Best Song By A UK Artist award at the NME Awards 2022, for "How Not To Drown." [1,2]

Caption: "Oh, Mother" (Song)
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry described "Oh, Mother" as the most depressing song on the album Vicious Creature. [1]
- "Oh, Mother" is the last song written for the Vicious Creature album, composed as a muted piano ballad. [2,3]
- "Oh Mother" was co-written by Mayberry and Dan McDougall (her principal Vicious Creature collaborator) in a single afternoon late in the recording sessions. Dan started playing a piano part; Mayberry initially tried to write another song about homesickness before Dan suggested something more personal. The full sketch was completed in a couple of hours. [2]
- The song was inspired by a family illness; Mayberry described thinking about it constantly while on tour: "When I go away on tour, I always think, 'Oh, is this the tour where I'm gone and I miss it'." [2,3]
- The song is structured chronologically, following Mayberry's relationship with her mother across different stages of her life — from the unquestioning love of childhood to adolescent disgust and, finally, the realisation that their time together is limited. The chronological narrative approach was Dan McDougall's suggestion; Mayberry stated she had never written a song with that kind of chronological structure before. [2]
- The lyric "It kills me to know you won't be around" appears in the song. [3]
- Dan McDougall described the writing session as involving "a lot of tears." Mayberry noted that the day before they had been recording "Something in the Air" — doing "shouts and screams in the studio" — and the next they were "having a wee sniffle next to the piano." [2]
- After the last studio session for the song, Mayberry and her collaborators went to Nando's: "But then we went to Nando's. So it's all about balance." [3]
- Mayberry stated "Oh, Mother" is a song she could never have written in CHVRCHES: "It's not a place that we would go emotionally or sonically." [3]
- Mayberry's mother was present in the audience at the Òran Mór Q&A on 2024-12-08 when this song was discussed. [2]

Caption: Lauren Mayberry Q&A at Òran Mór, Glasgow — 8 December 2024
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry took part in a Q&A session at Òran Mór, Glasgow, on 2024-12-08, hosted by BBC Scotland broadcaster Vic Galloway, in support of her debut solo album Vicious Creature. The event was presented in association with Assai Records. [1]
- The Òran Mór event was part of a "little instore tour" that Mayberry kicked off in Scotland; she said it "felt nice to come home first" for the opening stop, noting that many of the album's songs were "about things from the past." [1]
- Collaborator Dan (Mayberry's principal co-writer on Vicious Creature) was present at the Òran Mór event and performed with Mayberry; the instore format for this week featured only the two of them, stripped back. [1]
- Songs confirmed performed at the Òran Mór event include "Are You Awake?" and "Sunday Best." [1]
- Vic Galloway noted at the event that he and John Paul Mason ("JP") helped organise CHVRCHES' second-ever gig at Electric Circus, Edinburgh, under the pseudonym "Shark Week." Lauren confirmed: "We did, yes. And played about 6 in the evening if I remember." [1]
- Vic Galloway mentioned that CHVRCHES had done sessions for his BBC programme in both 2012 and 2014, showing Mayberry photographs from those sessions. [1]
- Mayberry confirmed at the Q&A that CHVRCHES had re-signed and were figuring out next steps: "Yes, so we've just taken a wee pause, but we've — the band's resigned and we're figuring out what to do." She added: "I feel like I should have like a tabard with a disclaimer on it, but I'm just going around at the moment like, sorry, it's fine, it'll happen again." [1]
- Upcoming spring 2025 shows announced at the time of the Q&A included Glasgow Barrowlands and La Belle Angèle in Edinburgh; Mayberry noted those shows would feature "loud drums and synths and guitar and bass and everything" in contrast to the stripped-back instore format. [1]

Caption: "Out Of My Head" feat. KOM_I / Wednesday Campanella (2018)
Facts:
- "Out Of My Head" is a collaborative track featuring KOM_I (コムアイ) of Japanese act Wednesday Campanella (水曜日のカンパネラ); it was released digitally on August 3, 2018, accompanied by a full-animation music video. [1]
- The physical release of "Out Of My Head feat. 水曜日のカンパネラ" is exclusive to the Love Is Dead Japan Deluxe Edition (HSE-6864, Hostess Entertainment, 14 September 2018); it is not physically available on any other edition. [1]
- Lauren Mayberry stated the following regarding the collaboration (translated from Japanese): "Japan has always remained a special place for CHVRCHES, and we are very happy to be able to deliver this special track to fans in Japan. KOM_I has a powerful and unique voice, and I deeply respect her as an individual. I am very honoured that she agreed to take on this musical collaboration." [1]
- Lauren Mayberry instructed KOM_I to "bring strong feelings, anger" when collaborating on "Out Of My Head" (per KOM_I's own statement, translated from Japanese). [1]
- KOM_I provided the following statement about the collaboration (translated from Japanese; source is a Japanese-language product page): "Lauren from CHVRCHES told me to unleash strong feelings, anger! But at 25 years old now, with things coming together around me, I find that I'm less and less driven to the point of tears from anger. But even though I should have gotten freedom, I still can't change the world, I can't feel at ease in life — and to this suspended, uncertain self and this era, I felt like giving some encouragement, so I wrote it. Kawasaki and Glasgow, Kiyoshiro Imawano and David Bowie, edamame and fish & chips." [Original Japanese: 川崎とグラスゴー、忌野清志郎とデヴィッド・ボウイ、枝豆とフィッシュ＆チップス。] [1]

Caption: "Over" — CHVRCHES Non-Album Track, February 2023
Facts:
- A non-album CHVRCHES track, "Over," was released in February 2023, announced concurrently with news of the band's Island Records deal. [1,2,4]
- "Over" was released on 2023-02-24 on Virgin EMI in Australia, per the australian-charts.com singles discography. Virgin EMI is the Universal Music Group imprint operating in the UK/Australian market, consistent with the Island Records deal announced in the same week. [3]
- "Over" traces back to 2017 demo sessions in which Martin Doherty and producer/songwriter Oscar Holter would hang out and write demos together — described by Doherty as a casual creative period: "We were working on some stuff just for fun." Those sessions preceded Holter's run of major credits including The Weeknd's "Blinding Lights" and "Save Your Tears" and Coldplay & BTS's "My Universe." [4]
- The band knew "Over" could be a big song for them, but the demo sat unfinished for years because it didn't fit the self-produced ethos of Screen Violence (2021), which the trio wanted to write and produce entirely on their own. At the end of 2022 they reconnected with Holter, who wanted to revisit the track; they worked it into its released form. Doherty: "got it to a point where everyone was happy with it... something that felt 2023, and not like something that's been kicking around for a few years." [4]
- "Over" was CHVRCHES' first standalone single in approximately ten years — not tethered to an album or an external collaboration. Doherty: "There's something incredibly freeing and no-strings about thinking outside of a long-form narrative, for the first time in 10 years... It's quite liberating, and quite fun." [4]
- Billboard describes "Over" as "a more muscular version of Chvrches' synth-rock sound with a classic Mayberry hook designed for expansive festival crowds... the percussion recalls a classic Jam & Lewis beat." (Journalistic characterisation by Jason Lipshutz, Billboard.) [4]
- The "Over" music video was filmed near Byron Bay, Australia, while CHVRCHES were on tour there; the band had one evening to shoot the video at a stone structure with glass panels and lighting shining through them from underneath, during a torrential rainstorm that was not part of the original plan. [5]
- The rain forced last-minute changes: Mayberry had planned wavy hair but the wet conditions caused it to expand uncontrollably, requiring her to pivot to a slicked-back look inspired by Tallulah from the film *Bugsy Malone*: "I had to have a frank conversation with the makeup artists to reset. 'We're just going to stick it down. Just glue it to your head.' We went for a Tallulah from *Bugsy Malone*, slicked-back look." [5]
- "Over" was the first CHVRCHES music video to feature all three members filming together in person since *Love Is Dead* (2018); all *Screen Violence* music videos were shot with each member filming separately on green screen. [5]
- The visual aesthetic for "Over" was deliberately designed to depart from the *Screen Violence* era's specific colour palette and textures, and to bridge visually toward the TBOWYTB 10th anniversary aesthetic: Mayberry stated "we thought maybe it's time to move back to a more graphic universe so that this miniature chapter can lead into that chapter." [5]

Caption: "Punch Drunk" — Lauren Mayberry Solo Track
Facts:
- "Punch Drunk" was the first song co-written by Lauren Mayberry and Dan (her principal Vicious Creature collaborator). Dan began the track with a bassline that Mayberry said immediately felt unlike anything else on the record; she described the song as "quite odd." [1]
- Vic Galloway compared "Punch Drunk" to David Bowie's "Fashion," observing that it "comes from that place." Mayberry and Dan acknowledged the resemblance; Mayberry joked: "Teaching us what we ripped off, like." [1]
- Mayberry described the song as "probably quite fictional, or it's like it's about a true thing and then you can put a fun storytelling lens on it." [1]

Caption: CHVRCHES Recover EP, March 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- The Recover EP was released in March 2013. [1]
- "Recover" debuted atop Billboard's Hot Singles Sales chart in March 2013. [5]
- The band had sold 14,000 copies of the three-song "Recover" single since its March 2013 release, according to Nielsen SoundScan (as of 2013-09). [5]
- Martin Doherty described the relief at having a physical release in a 2013 Anchor Shop TV interview: "it feels like a relief on a lot of levels to finally have a physical release — a legitimate release — out in the world, because it feels like we've existed for too long on the internet and bands can only survive for so long on that before they start really moving forward and being a band in the traditional way." He added he was "excited by the idea that someone can go into a shop and actually buy a record or a CD." [2]
- Paste Magazine's March 2013 feature describes "Recover" as "their third single" [in singles chronology], noting that by the time it was released "it was nearly unmissable" — reflecting its status as the band's most-anticipated release to date after the organic growth of their first two singles. [3]
- The australian-charts.com singles discography records an Australian commercial single release of "Recover" (Vertigo) on 2013-02-22, predating the March EP release by approximately two to four weeks; this likely reflects an advance digital single release in the Australian market, with the full Recover EP following in March. [4]
- The Recover EP comprised three original tracks ("Recover", "Now Is Not the Time", "ZVVL") and two remixes of "Recover" (by CID RIM and Curxes). [6,8]
- CID RIM, who contributed a remix of "Recover" to the Recover EP, is an Austrian music producer. [8]
- Curxes, who contributed a remix of "Recover" to the Recover EP, are a UK-based act. [8]
- The Recover EP was released on 2013-03-26 in North America, per a Billboard trade article published 2013-03-04 announcing the upcoming release. [6]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: a Chromewaves roundup published 2013-03-08 stated the EP would be "issued digitally on March 25" — one day earlier; this may reflect a different regional window or a minor scheduling change. [7]
- The title track "Recover" was set to A-side a 12″ vinyl release for Record Store Day on 2013-04-20. [7]
- A music video for "Recover" had been released by approximately 2013-03-08, characterised as "just released" in a Chromewaves roundup of that date. [7]
- CHVRCHES chose CID RIM and Curxes as remixers for the Recover EP because they were "artists we'd like to work with" (Mayberry) and "artists we appreciate" (Doherty). [9]
- Doherty cited CID RIM's self-titled 2012 album as one of his favourite records at the time; the remix invitation was "a total no-brainer": "We asked him, and when he said yes, we were flattered and delighted." [9]
- CHVRCHES discovered Curxes in part through their "similarly bad command of spelling" — a playful reference to CHVRCHES' own V/U letter substitution in their band name (Doherty, April 2013). [9]
- All three CHVRCHES members made Hitchcock-style cameo appearances in the "Recover" music video; Mayberry described the conceit: "we are looking like we are making an artistic statement and also we don't have to be in them yet." [9]

Caption: CHVRCHES Perform at Royal Albert Hall for the First Time, 2016
Facts: - In 2016, CHVRCHES performed at Royal Albert Hall for the first time. [1]

Caption: Screen Violence — Recording Sessions, 2020–2021
Facts:
- The genesis of Screen Violence was established before the pandemic hit, in February 2020: Mayberry stated the band "got a lot of the initial ideas and concepts going... we kind of had a blueprint to work off and some demos there." [1]
- The vast majority of Screen Violence was recorded with all three members completely separate from each other, due to social distancing and travel restrictions; they only came together for "a couple of weeks" at the end of production to put the finishing touches on the album. [1]
- Doherty described the extended solo studio time during lockdown as enabling more experimentation: "In the past, if I was making an album, I would have one eye on the clock or one eye on the tour schedule... on this album, I was like, 'Oh, what's inside the guitar pedal? Let me open that up.' It was a very immersive experience for me, from my perspective of things." He added: "You're not going to a studio every day, you're not going to the pub afterwards... I feel like most of my day was spent doing something to do with the record." [1]
- Mayberry described the pandemic recording experience as "making the record in a vacuum again, because the outside world ceased to exist, for the most part," comparing it to recording the first album because "you make your first record in a vacuum because nobody knows who your band is." She stated she "wouldn't want to do it again." [1]
- The band were insistent they did not want to make a "pandemic record." Doherty stated: "I can't be any less interested in this, in the sound of people's boredom," explicitly distinguishing Screen Violence from Taylor Swift's Folklore and Charli XCX's How I'm Feeling Now, which he characterised as albums "born out of and obsessed with the introspection that comes with being cut off from the rest of the world." [1]
- Mayberry stated that many of the lyrics and ideas originated before the pandemic, but that the nature of 2020 "enhanced" the violent imagery somewhat. She added: "I can't imagine trying to make a fucking upbeat dance pop record in summer 2020." [1]
- "Screen Violence" was once conceived as the band's name before being adopted as the album title. [2]
- The album was produced and mixed entirely in-house by Lauren Mayberry, Martin Doherty, and Iain Cook, working remotely between Los Angeles and Glasgow via video calls and shared audio files. The Beat Ink Japan promotional page (translated from Japanese) provides the most explicit statement that mixing was also self-handled: "ミックスまで本人たちで行った" ("they even handled the mixing themselves"). [2,3]
- Mayberry stated at the time of the album announcement: "I think for me it was helpful to go into the process with the idea that I could write something escapist almost. That felt freeing initially, to have concepts and stories to weave your own feelings and experiences through but in the end, all the lyrics were definitely still personal." [2]
- Doherty stated at the time of the album announcement: "To me, the screen aspect was a bit more literal. When we were making the record, it was like half of our lives were lived through screens. What began as a concept was now a lifeline." [2]
- In a May 2019 interview — the earliest corpus record of Mayberry publicly acknowledging plans for a fourth album — she confirmed the band intended to record again after wrapping the Love Is Dead touring cycle: "Yeah, we don't have any dates or plans yet. I think we're going to be rolling on touring until the autumn time, and then take stock of what we're doing. But yeah, I think, hopefully, we don't know when. But if they'll let us do it, then I think we're going to do it." The fourth album, Screen Violence, was released in 2021. [4]
- The remote collaborative writing and recording sessions for Screen Violence routed audio through a software programme called Audio Movers, which allowed all three members to hear shared audio in real time over Zoom; because of this routing, Zoom's own video recording captured only silence — the audio existed for participants but was not recorded by Zoom. Mayberry explained: "you can't hear anything because the audio goes through this program called Audio Movers, so we can all hear it, but when you do the Zoom recording it's silent." [5]
- Screen Violence vocal sessions used a BOK 251 microphone — described by Martin Doherty as "a recreation of the famous 251 microphone by this boutique company called Bach, and it's the best-sounding microphone I've ever heard"; Iain Cook added it was "really great on Lauren's voice as well." [5]
- The heavy guitar presence on Screen Violence stemmed directly from Doherty experimenting with and building guitar pedals during the pandemic isolation period; Cook confirmed: "It came through the pandemic where Martin was digging into making pedals and stuff like that and experimenting with new guitar tones and that's basically the reason why there's so much guitar on Screen Violence. Once we got going, we just absolutely dug into it and really leaned into it and enjoyed being guitar players again." [6]
- Screen Violence was the first CHVRCHES album on which Mayberry wrote lyrics without holding herself back; on all previous records, the band never sat down to write with a specific message in mind — the song and melody always came first, with Mayberry adding lyrics to the finished music. [7]
- Doherty described the Screen Violence recording as the band becoming "a protective shell" from the outside world during the pandemic: "No one cared about us, because everyone was busy managing their own shit." He also called the result "the best thing we've ever done." [7]
- Screen Violence was mostly written and recorded over 2020–2021; Iain Cook remained in Glasgow throughout the sessions while Mayberry and Doherty were in America. [7]

Caption: Screen Violence (Album Release)
Facts:
- Screen Violence was self-produced by CHVRCHES, in contrast to Love Is Dead on which the band worked with A-list producers including Greg Kurstin. [1]
- Mayberry stated in October 2023 that the Screen Violence campaign was the point where CHVRCHES first moved substantially toward a more theatrical aesthetic in both the album's presentation and its live shows, though she noted it was still "trying to fit it into a pre-existing format." [2]
- Screen Violence recording began in February 2020, with initial ideas, concepts, and demos assembled before COVID-19 disrupted recording conditions; Doherty and Mayberry worked in Los Angeles and Cook worked in Glasgow throughout the sessions. [3,4]
- Screen Violence features a darker sound than previous CHVRCHES releases and includes a collaboration with Robert Smith of The Cure. [3]
- Screen Violence was released in August 2021 and landed at No. 4 on the Official U.K. Albums Chart upon release — a U.K. Top Five hit; AllMusic's biography describes it as "the group's fourth U.K. Top Ten album" [studio album]; The Skinny (April 2024) similarly states CHVRCHES had "racked up four UK top ten studio album releases." [3,4,10]
- Screen Violence was released in Australia on 2021-08-27 on the Vertigo label. It entered the Australian album chart on 2021-09-12, peaked at #6, and spent 2 weeks on the chart. [5]
- Screen Violence topped the charts in Scotland. [3]
- Screen Violence peaked at number 31 on the Billboard 200 Albums chart. [3]
- Screen Violence debuted on Billboard's Top Alternative Albums chart on 9/11/21, peaked at #5, and spent 1 week on the chart. [6]
- In the week of Screen Violence's UK release, Billboard reported that CHVRCHES were leading the UK Albums Chart race ahead of Maisie Peters and Kanye West (Lars Brandle, Billboard, 1 September 2021). [6]
- Brian De Palma and David Cronenberg were named by the band as cinematic reference points for Screen Violence. [4]
- Mayberry described Screen Violence as "more political than the last one," adding: "the songs weren't intended to be, but the personal is the political." [4]
- Screen Violence was released on Glassnote Records. [7]
- Gavin Lurssen served as mastering engineer for Screen Violence. [11]
- The album's narrative framework was described at release as "three screens" exploring "feelings of loneliness, disillusionment, fear, heartbreak and regret." [7]
- The Japan domestic CD edition of Screen Violence was released on 2021-08-27 (simultaneous with the global release) and includes a Robert Smith remix of "How Not To Drown" as bonus track 11, plus a lyric translation insert and notes booklet. The standard tracklist is: 1. Asking For A Friend; 2. He Said She Said; 3. California; 4. Violent Delights; 5. How Not To Drown (feat. Robert Smith); 6. Final Girl; 7. Good Girls; 8. Lullabies; 9. Nightmares; 10. Better If You Don't. [8]
- Screen Violence was available in Japan in multiple physical formats: domestic CD, a CD+T-shirt set, three vinyl formats, and cassette tape. First-press purchasers from BEATINK.COM received a signed postcard as a bonus. [8]
- Screen Violence debuted at #74 on the Billboard Canadian Albums chart (chart week 2021-09-11). [9]

Caption: CHVRCHES Screen Violence Tour (2021–2022)
Facts:
- A fall 2021 North American tour was announced simultaneously with the Screen Violence album reveal and the release of the second single "How Not to Drown" on 2021-06-03. [1]
- The Screen Violence live show featured costume changes and a fake blood effect — elements that Mayberry described as consistently baffling venue staff: "We did costume changes and there was, like, fake blood in the show and things like that. And basically every venue we went to, it was really baffling to everybody who worked in the venue because they were, like, 'Costume changes? People don't do costume changes.'" [2]
- Mayberry identified the theatrical work begun in the Screen Violence live show as a direct precursor to her decision to go fully theatrical as a solo artist: "What kind of became clear to me was like, 'Oh yeah, you love all this mad, silly s***, so maybe you should go do this mad silly s***." [2]
- Two consecutive sell-out nights at Barrowland Ballroom, Glasgow in 2023-06 were described as the tour reaching its "climax"; CHVRCHES then proceeded to Glastonbury. [3]
- The final Screen Violence headline show took place at Fat Sam's, Dundee on 2023-06-12 — the same venue at which the tour's planned UK-opening intimate show had been cancelled due to COVID-19 in 2022-03. [4]
- Donna Missal was the support act on the Screen Violence North American tour. [5,8]
- During the fall 2021 North American tour leg, Iain Cook tested positive for COVID and was quarantined at a hotel in Chicago; he rejoined the band in New York City for the final stretch of the tour. [5]
- CHVRCHES maintained strict COVID safety protocols throughout the 2021 North American tour: daily antigen tests, weekly PCR tests, and a dedicated staff member whose job was to ensure compliance. Mayberry confirmed on the record that other bands were hiding COVID cases from their crews, contrasting this with CHVRCHES' approach of spending extra money on protection: "I do know for a fact has happened in other bands and crews. No judgement though, because people are just trying to keep going and getting people paid." [5]
- The encore fake blood look was water-based FX makeup prepared in advance and applied by Mayberry in approximately 30 seconds before returning to stage. Mayberry cited Siouxsie Sioux as the inspiration for the theatrical horror aesthetic of the live show. [5]
- Audience response to the Screen Violence tour shows was notably more emotional than previous CHVRCHES tours; Mayberry observed more weeping in the crowd, leading her to actively avoid making eye contact with crying audience members to prevent losing her own composure. [5]
- During the Screen Violence North American tour, a couple got engaged during the changeover between Donna Missal's set and CHVRCHES' set at the Chicago show; Mayberry noted the band's pre-show horror-themed soundtrack made the occasion surreal: "in the changeover we play songs from or inspired by horror films, so it made for a magic moment for them!" [5]
- Jonny Scott was the live touring drummer throughout the Screen Violence cycle; he had first joined the CHVRCHES live touring lineup for the Love Is Dead campaign in 2018. [3,6,7,9]
- "He Said She Said" consistently opened CHVRCHES' set throughout the Screen Violence tour. [3,6,8,9]
- "Asking for a Friend" consistently opened the encore on the Screen Violence tour. [7,8,9]
- Consequence named CHVRCHES their "2021 Band of the Year." [5,7]
- The remaining 2022 tour dates as of 2022-08-03 were: 08/04 Boston MA (Roadrunner), 08/05 North Adams MA (Mass MoCA), 09/20 Denver CO (Mission Ballroom), 09/22 Salt Lake City UT (Twilight Concert Series), 09/24 Phoenix AZ (Marquee Theatre), 09/25 Los Angeles CA (Hollywood Bowl, co-headline with Grace Jones), 09/26 Sacramento CA (Ace of Spades), 09/28 Vancouver BC (Queen Elizabeth Theatre), 09/30 Seattle WA (Paramount Theatre), 10/01 Portland OR (Roseland Theater), 10/02 Spokane WA (Knitting Factory), 10/03 Boise ID (Knitting Factory) — the final show of the non-Australia/New Zealand run. [7]

Caption: "Shame" — Lauren Mayberry Solo Track
Facts:
- "Shame" is a Lauren Mayberry solo track co-written with Matthew Koma (referred to as "Matt" by Mayberry at the 2024-12-08 Q&A). [1,2]
- The Skinny (April 2024) described "Shame" as "dark synth-laden." [1]
- Mayberry described "Shame" as "one of the first ones that we wrote for the record." [2]
- The song's concept turns on the tagline "oh what a shame," which Mayberry said she worked into the track in a distinctive way. [2]
- Mayberry noted the song demands significant breath control in performance: "A lot of breath required — I don't know why I've done this." She also cited "Sunday Best" as the other track requiring similar stamina, saying she needed "new lungs" for both. [2]

Caption: CHVRCHES as "Shark Week" — Vic Galloway Event, Edinburgh, 2012
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- Prior to their first official show under the CHVRCHES name, the band performed under the pseudonym "Shark Week" at an event in Edinburgh co-organised by BBC Scotland broadcaster Vic Galloway and John Paul Mason ("JP"), held at Electric Circus, Edinburgh. [1,2]
- The show was performed at approximately 6pm. [2]
- The Shark Week show was not counted as an official CHVRCHES live appearance; by the reviewer's reckoning, the band's first official show was the Art School Glasgow concert in early July 2012. [1]
- Vic Galloway described the Electric Circus Edinburgh show as CHVRCHES' "second gig" — meaning it was the second show the band had ever played, not their first warm-up appearance. This is consistent with Under the Radar's claim that CHVRCHES played two warm-up gigs under the Shark Week billing: the Electric Circus show was the second of two pre-debut pseudonym shows, not the sole one. The first pseudonym show remains undocumented in this corpus. [2,3]

Caption: "Something in the Air" — Lauren Mayberry Solo Track
Facts:
- "Something in the Air" is the opening track on the Vicious Creature album; BBC describes it as channelling "the spirit of All Saints." [1,2]
- "Something in the Air" was co-written by Lauren Mayberry and Dan McDougall (her principal Vicious Creature collaborator) in London during the week of the 2024 UK general election (2024-07-04). [1]
- The red-white-and-blue megaphone imagery in the song is a direct reference to the UK general election week during which it was written. [1]
- During the London writing sessions, Mayberry and Dan encountered an unnamed British rock legend from the 1990s in the studio kitchen who was espousing 5G conspiracy theories. Mayberry declined to name him to avoid legal consequences; she noted he was wearing "a fancy Gucci bucket hat." The encounter directly influenced the creation of the song. [1]
- The song's structure opens with a spoken verse, a format Mayberry said she specifically sought: "I want to do like a kind of song — spoken verse thing — so that was what we started with." [1]
- "Something in the Air" was released as a single ahead of the Vicious Creature album. [1]
- Mayberry named "Something in the Air" as her favourite song on Vicious Creature; she described first performing it live before its release and texting Dan when audiences responded at the chorus: "they're fucking get that one right, I'm going to do another one." [1]

Caption: "Sorry Etc." — Lauren Mayberry Solo Track
Facts:
- BBC describes the sonic character of "Sorry Etc." as "a chaotic hybrid of garage rock and drum & bass." [2]
- Paul Gallagher — described by Mayberry as CHVRCHES' long-standing front-of-house engineer and "an amazing musician, producer, and a very good bass player" — plays bass on the recorded version of "Sorry Etc." [1]
- The recording of Paul Gallagher on bass came about after Mayberry discussed the song with him after a show during the 2023 pre-release tour. Gallagher described what the bassline should sound like; his description matched exactly what Mayberry was looking for, so she asked him to play it himself. Mayberry described the result as "a very nice little circle thing" — bringing the front-of-house engineer into the record. [1]
- "Sorry Etc." was performed on Mayberry's 2023 pre-release solo tour before the Vicious Creature album was released; during those shows, the bassline was performed differently — Mayberry described it as "a big beefy bassline" that she felt needed to be "heavier." [1]
- The song was written after Mayberry and a collaborator spent a day unsuccessfully working on a different track; pivoting to a new idea produced "Sorry Etc." [1]
- The song's refrain consists of Mayberry shout-singing "I'm sorry, et cetera" on repeat, as first documented in the 9:30 Club performance on 2023-09-04. [3]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Spilt Milk Festival 2019
Facts:
- CHVRCHES were confirmed as headliners for Spilt Milk 2019 across two dates: Commonwealth Park, Canberra ACT on Saturday 23 November 2019, and Victoria Park, Ballarat VIC on Saturday 30 November 2019. [1,2,3]
- The Ballarat leg of Spilt Milk 2019 was the first time the festival expanded to Victoria. [1]
- Victoria Park, Ballarat was set to accommodate 25,000 festival-goers for Spilt Milk 2019, described as roughly the same size as Groovin The Moo's Canberra leg and around half the size of Splendour in the Grass. [1]
- Tickets for both Spilt Milk 2019 dates sold out after going on sale on Thursday 13 June 2019; a waitlist was scheduled to open on 11 July 2019. [1]
- Other notable acts on the Spilt Milk 2019 lineup included Juice WRLD, Khalid, Illy, Ocean Alley, Dune Rats, Golden Features, Middle Kids, Mansionair, G Flip, Tones And I, Confidence Man, and Psychedelic Porn Crumpets. [1]
- Spilt Milk 2019 was the fourth edition of the festival, which had previously sold out within under 30 minutes in every preceding year. [3]
- Pre-sale tickets for Spilt Milk 2019 were released at 8am on 2019-06-12 (Wednesday), with general tickets going on sale at 8am on 2019-06-13 (Thursday). [3]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Splendour in the Grass, 2018
Facts: - CHVRCHES performed at Splendour in the Grass on 2018-07-21; the Sydney Hordern Pavilion sideshow on 2018-07-22 was presented as a follow-up to their Splendour set. [1,2,3]

Caption: "Such Great Heights" (CHVRCHES Single, February 2026)
Facts:
- "Such Great Heights (CHV Version)" was released in Australia as a commercial single on 2026-02-17 on Hollywood Records, per the australian-charts.com singles discography; the band's own Instagram confirmed global digital availability on the same date: "out everywhere now." [1,3]
- CHVRCHES covered The Postal Service's "Such Great Heights" for the season 3 finale of *Tell Me Lies* (Hulu/Disney+); the cover was recorded during the studio sessions for their forthcoming fifth studio album. [2]
- Before recording the cover, Lauren Mayberry emailed Ben Gibbard directly to ensure The Postal Service "wouldn't find it weird and so that he didn't think we were being creepy"; she stated he was "characteristically lovely about it." [2]
- Mayberry described the creative approach to the cover: "Such Great Heights is basically a perfect pop song and I don't know how one would ever top that, so we just tried to be respectful and faithful to it. I like to sit with the lyrics when we're doing a cover and think about what story they're telling and what they mean to me." [2]
- Mayberry stated she "always liked the balance of joy and melancholy in the Postal Service's music" and cited Ben Gibbard and Jenny Lewis as "probably two of my favourite lyricists of all time." [2]
- CHVRCHES described the cover in their 2026-02-17 official Instagram announcement as "one of our favourite songs, by one of our favourite bands, for one of our favourite TV shows." [3]

Caption: CHVRCHES at Summer Sonic, Japan, August 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- CHVRCHES performed at Summer Sonic in Japan; the Hostess Entertainment product page (September 2018) mentions this as part of their breakthrough in Japan following the release of The Bones of What You Believe in September 2013. The specific year(s) of their Summer Sonic appearance(s) are not stated in this source. [1]
- The SXSW 2016 official CHVRCHES artist bio lists Summer Sonic in Japan among the band's "signature appearances" during the TBOWYTB touring period (2013–2014), corroborating the TBOWYTB era date. [2]
- CHVRCHES performed at Summer Sonic on two consecutive days in 2013: 2013-08-10 at the Tokyo venue and 2013-08-11 at the Osaka venue, per the June 2013 tour announcement. [3]

Caption: "Sunday Best" (Song)
Facts:
- Lauren Mayberry described "Sunday Best" as slightly cheerful during a 9:30 Club performance on 2025-02-18. [1]
- "Sunday Best" was co-written by Lauren Mayberry and Greg Kurstin, who produced nine tracks on CHVRCHES' third studio album Love Is Dead. Mayberry described it as "really nice to go back and work with somebody that you know so well, but it's such a different scenario." [2]
- When Mayberry described the '90s references she wanted for the song, Kurstin responded: "Here's your Fat Boy Slim piano." [2]
- Mayberry was deliberately conscious of not letting the album sound too American, and acknowledged Kurstin is American but said: "we forgive him because he's worked with so many British artists." She stated Kurstin immediately understood her reference points when she described them. [2]
- Mayberry cited "Sunday Best" alongside "Shame" as a track requiring exceptional stamina to perform live: "That one and Sunday Best — I need new lungs." [2]
- "Sunday Best" was performed live at the Òran Mór Q&A in Glasgow on 2024-12-08. [2]

Caption: CHVRCHES at South by Southwest, Austin, Texas, 2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- After signing with Glassnote in January 2013, CHVRCHES embarked on an international tour that included a performance at South by Southwest (SXSW) in Austin, Texas. [1]
- CHVRCHES won South by Southwest's inaugural Grulke Prize for Developing Non-U.S. Act at SXSW 2013; SXSW confirmed this was CHVRCHES' US and SXSW debut as a band. Doherty's reaction: "That was kind of overwhelming. It came out of the blue. We had not heard of it before, so when we found out, we were taken aback. It's a really nice thing." The other recipients at SXSW 2013 were The Flaming Lips (Career Act) and Haim (Developing U.S. Act). [2,4,6,7,8]
  - ⚠️ Refines: Consequence of Sound (2013-03-27, [6]) named the award "Grulke Prize for Best Non-U.S. Act" — the official SXSW press release (2013-03-20, [8]) confirms the correct category name is "Developing Non-US Act."
- The SXSW 2013 Grulke Prize was the inaugural edition, developed in honor of SXSW Creative Director Brent Grulke, who passed away on 2012-08-13. Jurors were music critics, industry professionals, and SXSW staff who knew and worked with Grulke personally. [8]
- CHVRCHES made five packed performances at SXSW in March 2013. [5]
- CHVRCHES performed at the Hype Hotel at SXSW 2013. [7]
- CHVRCHES performed at a late-night show at Red 7 Patio at SXSW 2013. [7]
- At the Stubb's SXSW show, Kendrick Lamar and Solange performed after CHVRCHES on the same bill; Doherty described the backstage proximity as "kind of overwhelming" and said he "hid in the corner"; Mayberry noted the band's tactic was to take stealthy photos from a distance rather than approach them directly. [7]
- At SXSW, Mayberry watched Blue Hawaii perform on the first night; Cook attended a Savages set, which he described as "one of the most engaging live acts I've seen in years"; Mayberry watched the Kathleen Hanna documentary *The Punk Singer* on the first day at SXSW, skipping an afternoon nap before a show scheduled at 1am — she regretted the choice by show time. [7]
- CHVRCHES made their New York City debut later in March 2013, following SXSW. [5]
- CHVRCHES flew directly from Los Angeles to Austin, Texas for SXSW the morning after their first-ever LA show in March 2013; the two dates were consecutive, with the SF–LA–Austin routing confirmed in the Paste Magazine interview conducted in Echo Park on the evening before the LA show. [3]

Caption: The Bones of What You Believe — 10th Anniversary Deluxe Edition, October 2023
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- In October 2023, CHVRCHES celebrated the tenth anniversary of The Bones of What You Believe's release with a deluxe edition via Glassnote Records. [1,4]
- The TBOWYTB 10th Anniversary Edition was confirmed for release on 13 October 2023 via Glassnote. [2]
  - ⚠️ Conflicts with: the BroadwayWorld announcement article (published 2023-10-20) describes the Special Edition as released "today," placing the release date at 2023-10-20; the two dates may reflect different regional or format release windows — [4]
- The Special Edition is available in the following formats: 1xLP clear vinyl, 2xLP black vinyl with die-cut sleeve, 2xCD, and Digital; the 2xLP format includes an exclusive 7" vinyl featuring two unheard demos of "The Mother We Share." [4]
- The TBOWYTB 10th anniversary deluxe edition was remastered by Gavin Lurssen, who also mastered Screen Violence (2021). [1,4]
- The TBOWYTB 10th anniversary deluxe edition included four previously unheard songs and five live tracks (the live tracks recorded at Ancienne Belgique in 2013), alongside the remastered original 12-track album. [1,4]
- One of the previously unheard songs, "City On Fire," was released publicly for the first time on the Special Edition's release date. [4]
- Lauren Mayberry stated in the press release accompanying the Special Edition: "It feels quite strange that Bones is almost a decade old. In some ways, it's like it just happened, but also like that era was a lifetime ago. We are very grateful to all the fans who gave that album a special place in their heart, and still show us so much kindness today." [4]
- The anniversary edition includes "a couple of alternate versions" of "The Mother We Share," described by Doherty as "just for fun" — though he noted there are "even weirder versions than those." [3]
- A Stereogum track-by-track retrospective interview with all three band members was published to accompany the 10th anniversary; in it all three described reflecting on each song on TBOWYTB for the first time in a decade. [3]

Caption: The Bones of What You Believe — Recording Sessions, Glasgow, 2011–2013
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- TBOWYTB was recorded at Alucard Studios, a basement flat on the south side of Glasgow, which Iain Cook was initially renting for his film and TV scoring work; the band used it out of necessity, having no other studio resources at the time. Cook: "We did the first album there out of necessity." [1]
- The 12 songs on The Bones of What You Believe were written and recorded "between late 2011 and early summer 2012," establishing that the core recording was complete well before the July 2012 debut live show (at which the album was described as "already fully recorded and finished"). [6]
- The core hardware synthesizer setup during TBOWYTB recording consisted of three synths: a Moog Voyager, a Prophet 08, and a Roland Juno 106. Doherty: "We had two synths at the time, the Moog Voyager and the Prophet 08." The Juno 106 was added for bass work. [1]
- Additional software synthesizers were used during the TBOWYTB sessions, including Arturia products (including their Minimoog emulation), Korg Legacy, and GForce software. Doherty used the GForce Minimonsta — a lightweight Minimoog emulation — because his computer was too slow to run the more CPU-intensive Arturia equivalent, particularly with soft clipping enabled. [1]
- Beat programming on TBOWYTB was done using a sample library of classic drum-machine sounds rather than hardware drum machines. [1]
- The majority of bass parts on TBOWYTB were created using a Roland Juno 106 with the sub-octave "ramped right, right up." Cook: "The chorus module in it really brings out the top. Nice fat bass. An incredibly stable sub. I think all of the big sub lifts are between that and the Moog." [1]
- During TBOWYTB sessions, the Moog Voyager was frequently routed through Iain Cook's guitar pedals — including an Electro-Harmonix Memory Man delay and a Holy Grail reverb — partly to compensate for sounds that were not quite sitting right on their own. Doherty: "But it was all covering up the fact that we were still finding our feet, layer upon layer." [1]
- Vocals for TBOWYTB were recorded using an Advanced Audio CM48 — a U47 clone — positioned inside an SE Electronics Reflexion Filter, in the same room as the recording equipment; there was no dedicated vocal booth. Mayberry: "I guess now we complain about our little foam vocal booth, but we didn't have anything on the first record." [1]
- The Neumann KH120A monitors in the main Alucard room were used during both TBOWYTB and Every Open Eye recording; however, a Tannoy sub was paired with them during TBOWYTB, later upgraded to a matched Neumann sub for EOE. [1]
- The DAW used for TBOWYTB recording sessions was Steinberg Nuendo; the band later switched to Cubase for Every Open Eye, feeling Nuendo had too many features unnecessary for their workflow. [1]
- Rich Costey served as mix engineer for The Bones of What You Believe, chosen for his rock production pedigree. Doherty: "We specifically went to a guy with a rock pedigree because, to us, we're still an indie band using keyboards, instead of an electronic dance outfit. We feel like an indie band, we write like an indie band. But his classic thing when you got in the room with him was, 'Don't look at the meters, man, listen to the thing.'" [1]
- The TBOWYTB production approach involved stacking multiple synthesizer layers, using sounds to cover for sonic imperfections rather than addressing them directly. Doherty: "This good sound on top of this good sound we thought equated to one great sound. You've got to go there and make those mistakes to realise that's not really how it works. We were still finding our feet as electronic musicians. We were still basically indie guys delving into electronic music for the first time in a serious way." [1]
- Early versions of "The Mother We Share" were "so densely packed full of instrumental ideas" and "so loud and compressed" — Cook and Doherty repeatedly tried to strip them out to let the song breathe. The verse section of the song was originally a post-chorus; structural changes to "The Mother We Share" continued right up to the point of release. [2]
- Cook and Doherty wrote sitting side-by-side at the desk, throwing ideas down to see what stuck. There was always an MPC on the desk, used at the start of the writing process to determine feel and tempo — "it'd be how we'd decide if a song was too fast or too slow, or needed half-time or double-time." [2]
- Doherty specifically named the Juno-106 and the Moog Voyager as "the cornerstone of the whole album" — the first time he had played around with hardware synthesizers in the studio: "There was something so new and exciting about being able to run up to the synthesizer and touch it and make something I would have never gotten sitting at the computer." [2]
- "We Sink" was built around a Prophet '08 sequence that starts the track and "a nice four-on-the-floor kick" — Cook described the track's construction as being in "the mode of being dance producers rather than trying to write a pop song." [2]
- The drum reverb on "Night Sky" used an AMS RMX16 — the same unit used on Kate Bush and Peter Gabriel records — to recreate the tom explosion sound from "Hounds of Love": "The reverb on the drums was the AMS [RMX16] that was used on a lot of Kate Bush and Peter Gabriel records, and we wanted to do that tom explosion sound on 'Hounds Of Love.'" (Doherty) [2]
- The cut-up vocal samples in the bridge of "Night Sky" were created using a small MPC50: "We just loaded a bunch of Lauren's vocal samples and mapped it out. It was just a case of us jamming on the pads and coming up with sections that could be repeated riffs." (Cook) The same jam-on-the-pads approach was used for the vocal riff on "The Mother We Share." [2]
- "Night Sky" was in the second batch of songs the band wrote; it was played at their first-ever show. [2]
- The bass on "Lungs" layers a distorted bass guitar with a Moog synthesizer playing the same part: "We doubled up the bass guitar with a synthesizer playing the same part... We had a Moog behind that really distorted bass guitar — that's a Nine Inch Nails thing." (Cook/Doherty) [2]
- "Lungs" originally contained a Nicki Minaj sample, but CHVRCHES had Lauren Mayberry replace it with her own vocal sample: "that was the kind of major label artist that would come after you." (Doherty) [2]
- The exhale sound at the start of "Lungs" is Martin Doherty at the end of a vocal take; Cook and Doherty turned it into a percussive element: "That's me at the end of the vocal take, being like, 'That was a tough one.'" (Doherty) [2]
- Some lyrics in "Science/Visions" were "almost straight lifts from the Tibetan Book of the Dead": "I was just running out of ideas and flipping through and was like, 'Maybe if we just rephrase this slightly, it'll sound mysterious and cool.'" (Cook) Doherty described it as his favourite song on the album. [2]
- The lyrics for "Recover" were written by Cook and Doherty; the song is about paper fortune tellers — Cook: "'open the envelope' means 'on the synth'" (disputed by Cook himself as a joke). The studio chatter at the opening of the released track is Lauren Mayberry saying "That one's a bit tuney." The song resonated particularly with people who had been through substance problems. [2]
- Lauren Mayberry wrote the lyrics for "Under the Tide"; Doherty sang the song because it was in a key too awkward for Mayberry — "It was in a weird key for you. So instead of repitching the whole thing, I sang it." This was not a preplanned decision. [2]
- Iain Cook stated that CHVRCHES deliberately aim for a lyrical balance of apparent pop accessibility and underlying "menace" and "melancholy," citing Kate Bush as a primary reference: "a lot of our songs and lyrics have [menace and melancholy]. Even though they might seem quite poppy... they're actually a lot darker and sadder. Which is a difficult balance to get. But something that we definitely try to do as much as possible." [3]
- Mayberry described the vocal production philosophy that shaped the TBOWYTB sound: "My vocals are kind of quite sweet. If the music was produced in a certain way, it could be horribly saccharine and awful. The fact we have that sweetness in the vocals means you can go a bit further — making it a bit darker, a bit dirtier." [4]
- As of December 2012, the band had 18 songs in various states of completion in Cook's basement studio. Cook: "We have 18 songs in various states." He planned to finish all existing material by the end of 2013-01, then use 2013-02 to write additional songs: "We're going to try and finish all of this material by the end of January and then take February to come up with a couple more." [4]
- To maintain productivity, the band kept "office hours wherever possible" in Glasgow ("it's good to have a regime," Doherty). Someone had set up a shared Google document for the band to suggest album title ideas; as of December 2012, Cook said it was "completely devoid of text." [4]
- Doherty described the core songwriting approach: rather than someone bringing in a finished song, a track would begin with a piece of equipment, a snare sound, or something unusual, and the band would "build from a weird loop" — "not necessarily from a basic song structure in the beginning, but building it from a smaller idea than that." Mayberry added that being able to demo throughout gave them a useful record of their progress: "it's quite good to have a record of what you've been doing." [5]
- During the TBOWYTB recording sessions, the band took breaks to collectively watch Prince and Whitney Houston videos online as a way of keeping morale up in the studio. [7]
- By early 2013, CHVRCHES had re-recorded all of their demo recordings in anticipation of the full-length debut album. [7]
- As of 2013-04-02, CHVRCHES had completed tracking on The Bones of What You Believe; Doherty stated: "We've actually finished tracking on what we perceive as the album. So, the pressure is sort of off at least for that part." [8]
- As of 2013-04-02, three further weeks of studio writing time remained after tracking was complete, with Doherty noting: "Everyone keeps saying to me that's when the exciting stuff will happen." [8]
- The band planned to release one further single or "some music on the internet" before the album came out in September 2013; Doherty: "It's important for us to release things at the right time and not disappear for too long." [8]

Caption: The Bones of What You Believe (Album Release)
Era: The Bones of What You Believe
Facts:
- The Bones of What You Believe had staggered regional release dates: australian-charts.com records the Australian release as 2013-09-20; other corpus sources cite 2013-09-23 (likely UK); AllMusic lists 2013-09-24 with a total running time of 48:00 (likely US/international); a June 2013 US tour announcement article corroborates the September 24th date for the US market. These dates are consistent with a regional release window rather than a data conflict. [1, 2, 20]
- The album title "The Bones of What You Believe" comes from a lyric in the song "Strong Hand"; according to Mayberry, it refers to the raw "creativity and effort" that served as the skeleton for the full-length. [15]
- NPR streamed The Bones of What You Believe beginning 2013-09-17, ahead of the official release. [15]
- The album was recorded at Glasgow's Alucard Studio using a number of synths, including a Minimoog Voyager. [1, 3]
- AllMusic categorises The Bones of What You Believe under the genre Pop/Rock with the following style tags: Indie Electronic, Synth Pop, Alternative/Indie Rock, Left-Field Pop, Pop. [1]
- The complete 12-track listing with individual running times as listed on AllMusic is: (1) "The Mother We Share" (3:12); (2) "We Sink" (3:34); (3) "Gun" (3:53); (4) "Tether" (4:46); (5) "Lies" (3:41); (6) "Under the Tide" (4:32); (7) "Recover" (3:45); (8) "Night Sky" (3:51); (9) "Science/Visions" (3:58); (10) "Lungs" (3:02); (11) "By the Throat" (4:09); (12) "You Caught the Light" (5:37). [1]
- "The Mother We Share," "Gun," and "Recover" had each appeared on earlier CHVRCHES EPs before being included on The Bones of What You Believe. [1]
- The album arrived after approximately a year of anticipation, during which CHVRCHES built pre-release buzz through blogger support, several EPs, and inclusion on the BBC Sound of 2013 shortlist. [1]
- Martin Doherty takes lead vocal on "You Caught the Light"; AllMusic describes it as one of "keyboardist/vocalist Martin Doherty's lead turns" and characterises it as the "dreamy album-closer." [1]
- "Under the Tide" and "You Caught the Light" are the two Doherty lead-vocal tracks on The Bones of What You Believe; confirmed by Doherty, they close out sides A and B respectively — a deliberate sequencing choice. Doherty stated the band is "meticulous with sequencing" and does not "buy into the idea that the album, as a format, is over." [4]
